cards.front: How do adapters register themselves as plugins?
cards.back: Via an @adapter decorator that registers the class into a global registry at import time. Third-party adapters can register out-of-tree through Python setuptools entry-points under the shared `geosim.plugins` group.

cards.front: What is gravity as a survey method, and what is the gravity anomaly?
cards.back: Gravity measures tiny variations in Earth's gravitational pull at the surface; denser rock pulls fractionally harder. The gravity anomaly is the measured pull minus the predicted "boring Earth" pull, serving as a proxy for buried density.

cards.front: What is a milligal (mGal)?
cards.back: The standard unit for gravity surveys, where 1 mGal = 10⁻⁵ m/s². The adapter declares it as the native unit because the platform's canonical storage unit may differ.

cards.front: In a gravity CSV adapter, what does the adapter pointedly NOT do?
cards.back: No call to pyproj (no reprojection), no ×1e-5 unit math (no conversion), and no database write. Coordinates leave in native UTM metres and values in mGal; the adapter's only transform contribution is the declarative SourceRef and units dict.

cards.front: What is gridding, and why is it never done automatically?
cards.back: Gridding (interpolation/stitching) turns scattered point measurements into a continuous field with a value at every location. It's never automatic because it's a modeling choice that invents values where nothing was measured — so raw Observations stay immutable, and gridding is a separate, user-initiated, parameter-recorded step producing a new derived Property Model.

cards.front: What CS analogy describes a gridded field versus the raw Observation?
cards.back: The raw Observation is the source data; a gridded field is a materialized view computed from it with explicit parameters. You keep both, and the view records the exact query that built it so it's reproducible.

cards.front: How does IDW (inverse-distance weighting) gridding work?
cards.back: A grid cell's value is the average of nearby data points, weighted by 1/dᵖ where d is distance and p is the power (default 2). It's fast and simple but carries no real uncertainty (uses a default noise floor).

cards.front: Compare the three main 2-D gridders: spline, kriging, IDW.
cards.back: Spline (the default, Green's-function, bias-corrected) fits a smooth surface and synthesizes an honest distance-growing σ. Kriging is the geostatistical gold standard giving a principled variogram-based variance. IDW is the simplest/fastest but gives no native uncertainty.

cards.front: Why mask gridded cells beyond the search radius to NaN instead of zero?
cards.back: Footprint honesty: a gridder must never paint values where there's no data to support them. Cells farther than max_distance from any station become NaN (the explicit "no data" sentinel) — never zero, never a nearest-edge bleed. The same NaN-not-zero discipline governs fusion.

cards.front: What is depth-of-investigation (DOI) and how does it affect 1-D soundings gridded into 3-D?
cards.back: DOI is the depth below which a sounding simply cannot see. When stitching 1-D soundings into a 3-D volume, cells below the local DOI are masked to NaN — the same footprint-honesty principle applied in depth.

cards.front: What are the three sub-stages of normalization?
cards.back: (a) Coordinates → the Engineering Frame (resolve vertical convention, then reproject); (b) Units → canonical; (c) 1-D/2-D → 3-D placement. All are delegated to the shared spatial framework and units registry, never reinvented per method.

cards.front: How does the normalizer resolve each z_convention into canonical elevation?
cards.back: elevation_up passes through (already canonical); depth_below_datum negates Z; depth_below_surface resolves against the surface model (a flat surface = negate plus a low-severity warning); MD needs a well deviation survey (flagged medium severity, treated as a vertical well until the survey arrives).

cards.front: How does normalization treat a missing source CRS in local vs georeferenced projects?
cards.back: In a local-mode project (no real-world georeferencing) coords are assumed already-Engineering and a stray source CRS is ignored with a warning. In a georeferenced project a missing source CRS is a hard error — the platform refuses to guess a real-world position.

cards.front: Why is a missing source unit treated as the most dangerous case in normalization?
cards.back: A silently-wrong unit is the worst failure mode of the whole system. The normalizer falls back to assuming the canonical unit but emits a HIGH-severity warning recorded in provenance — loud, never silent. (An unregistered property type, by contrast, is a hard error.)

cards.front: What happens when a value column arrives with no paired σ (uncertainty)?
cards.back: The normalizer applies the property type's default noise floor (a relative σ from the registry) and records the substitution. Measured data is never silently treated as error-free.

cards.front: How are 1-D, 2-D, and well-log data placed into 3-D at ingest?
cards.back: A 1-D sounding becomes a vertical column at its (x,y); a 2-D profile/section (ERT, 2-D seismic) is kept as a native vertical "curtain" `section` (not rasterized to voxels until the user resamples during fusion); a well log becomes a `wellcurve` Observation joined by wellId to a separate `wellPath` Feature. Placement only positions data — it stays raw.

cards.front: What is the >10%-bad-rows rule?
cards.back: If more than 10% of records are dropped, the import is escalated to `failed`. A few bad rows is normal field data, but losing a tenth of the file means something is structurally wrong and the import shouldn't be trusted. The 0.10 threshold is a default, overridable per upload.

cards.front: What is the "partial success over hard failure" principle in ingestion?
cards.back: Individual unparseable rows or corrupt traces are skipped, counted, and warned while the rest ingests. Hard failure (`failed`) is reserved for structural problems: corrupt header, unreadable file, undetectable method, unregistered property type, missing CRS in a georeferenced project, or >10% rows dropped.

cards.front: What is the IngestReport and its three terminal statuses?
cards.back: A structured run summary with primitive counts, a list of IngestWarnings (each with code, severity, and locus like "row 41"), and the drop ratio. Its terminal status is one of: ok, ok_with_warnings, or failed.

cards.front: How does the pipeline achieve idempotent re-ingestion?
cards.back: It's content-addressed: raw bytes are stored under their sha256 so identical bytes de-duplicate automatically. If the full idempotency key already exists in the catalog, the pipeline skips the parse and returns the existing dataset with reused=True.

cards.front: What four components make up the idempotency key?
cards.back: key = sha256(raw bytes) ‖ adapter.name ‖ adapter.version ‖ normalization params. Re-ingesting the same file with different params yields a new key and new derived primitives, but still shares the same raw file via content hash.

cards.front: What four things does each primitive's provenance edge record?
cards.back: (a) the raw file hash and path, (b) the adapter name and version, (c) the source CRS/datum/units before normalization, and (d) the normalization parameters (gridding method, variogram, search radius, …). This makes every transform auditable and lets a future re-projection replay from the raw bytes.

cards.front: How are corrections to already-ingested data handled, given observations are immutable?
cards.back: A corrected re-import is a new dataset linked to the prior one — never an in-place overwrite. Observations are immutable.

cards.front: What is the forward problem in geophysics?
cards.back: The forward problem is `model → data`: given a known earth, simulate what each sensor would read. It's deterministic physics, written $d = F(m)$, where $m$ is the model vector (one value per mesh cell) and $d$ is the predicted data vector (one per measurement). For a programmer, it's a pure function from a big array to a smaller array.

cards.front: What is the inverse problem, and why is it the direction we actually want?
cards.back: The inverse problem is `data → model`: given surface readings, recover the earth that produced them. We want it because we have data and want the subsurface — but it's fundamentally harder because $F$ usually has no clean inverse.

cards.front: Why must geophysics measure the Earth indirectly?
cards.back: You cannot see through rock, so you tie a sensor to some physics (gravity, electrical resistance, seismic travel time) and record surface readings, then connect those data back to the subsurface model.

cards.front: Which direction (forward/inverse) does the synthetic generator use vs. the inversion engines?
cards.back: The synthetic generator runs the forward direction (known earth → fake surveys) to manufacture realistic data; the inversion engines run the inverse direction (surveys → reconstructed earth).

cards.front: What is the data misfit term $\phi_d(m)$ and what does $W_d$ do?
cards.back: $\phi_d(m) = \lVert W_d(F(m) - d_{obs})\rVert^2$ measures how well predicted data matches observations. $W_d$ weights each datum by $1/\sigma$ (its measurement uncertainty), so noisier data counts less.

cards.front: What are the three reasons inversion is hard?
cards.back: (1) Under-determined — far more unknowns ($10^5$–$10^6$ cells) than measurements ($10^2$–$10^4$), so infinitely many models fit exactly (non-uniqueness). (2) Ill-posed/unstable — tiny data changes produce wildly different models. (3) Depth blindness — some physics (e.g. gravity) can't constrain depth.

cards.front: What is non-uniqueness in inversion?
cards.back: Because there are vastly more unknowns (mesh cells) than measurements, the system is under-determined — infinitely many models fit the data exactly. The data alone cannot single out one model.

cards.front: What does it mean that inversion is ill-posed / unstable?
cards.back: Tiny changes in noisy data can produce wildly different models. The naive least-squares solution amplifies noise into geologic-looking garbage — high-frequency 'checkerboard' artifacts.

cards.front: What is depth blindness, with the gravity example?
cards.back: Some physics has no intrinsic depth resolution. In gravity, a small shallow dense blob and a large deep one can produce the same surface anomaly, so the data doesn't constrain depth — the optimizer, left alone, smears everything to the surface.

cards.front: How is fitting only $\phi_d$ like overfitting in ML?
cards.back: It's like training a model with more parameters than data points and no regularization — it memorizes the noise and generalizes to nonsense. Inversion is also lossy reconstruction: the forward operator is a smoothing/low-pass operator that throws away information, so you can only recover a plausible earth, not the exact original.

cards.front: What is regularization in inversion, and what is the classic form?
cards.back: Since data alone can't pick one model, regularization adds a second term encoding what we expect an earth to look like (a prior). The classic form is Tikhonov, minimizing $\phi(m) = \phi_d(m) + \beta\,\phi_m(m)$.

cards.front: In $\phi(m) = \phi_d(m) + \beta\,\phi_m(m)$, what is each term?
cards.back: $\phi_d$ is data misfit (fit the measurements); $\phi_m$ is model regularization (penalize unreasonable models); $\beta$ is the trade-off knob — the single most important inversion parameter.

cards.front: What are the two components of the model regularization term $\phi_m$?
cards.back: Smallness — stay close to a reference model $m_{ref}$ (don't invent unjustified structure); and smoothness — penalize sharp jumps between neighboring cells (geology is mostly smooth). $\phi_m = \alpha_s\lVert m - m_{ref}\rVert^2 + \alpha_x\lVert\partial_x m\rVert^2 + \alpha_y\lVert\partial_y m\rVert^2 + \alpha_z\lVert\partial_z m\rVert^2$.

cards.front: What do the symbols $\alpha_s$ and $\alpha_{x,y,z}$ control in $\phi_m$?
cards.back: $\alpha_s$ weights smallness (closeness to the reference model); $\alpha_{x,y,z}$ weight smoothness along each axis. $\partial$ is a finite-difference derivative — the discrete gradient between adjacent cells.

cards.front: What happens when β is large vs. small?
cards.back: β large → regularization dominates → an overly smooth, featureless model that ignores the data (underfitting). β small → data misfit dominates → a noisy, overfit model chasing measurement noise.

cards.front: What is the discrepancy principle (χ target)?
cards.back: You fit data only to its noise level, not exactly. With data weighted by $1/\sigma$, a correct model's expected misfit is about the number of data points ($\chi \approx 1$ per datum). So β is cooled from large to small until $\phi_d$ reaches that target — explaining the signal but not the noise.

cards.front: What do SimPEG's BetaEstimate_ByEig, BetaSchedule, and TargetMisfit do?
cards.back: BetaEstimate_ByEig picks a starting β; BetaSchedule cools it each iteration; TargetMisfit stops at the χ target. Together they form a directive stack that automatically chooses β.

cards.front: What is the Tikhonov trade-off curve (L-curve)?
cards.back: A plot of the two objective terms as the inversion converges, streamed from live $(iteration, \phi_d, \phi_m, \beta)$ telemetry. It shows the trade-off between data misfit and model regularization as β cools.

cards.front: What is sensitivity (depth) weighting, and why is it needed for gravity?
cards.back: Gravity has no intrinsic depth resolution, so the optimizer piles structure at the surface. Depth weighting boosts the weight of deeper cells so they can hold structure, curing depth blindness. The same sensitivity info is reused as the model's uncertainty.

cards.front: What is 'the closure' — the core architectural bet of inversion?
cards.back: An inversion engine is just another PropertyModel producer: it consumes Observations + a model domain (mesh) and emits a PropertyModel with uncertainty and provenance — the same primitive the platform already stores, resamples, fuses, and visualizes. So no new downstream machinery is needed, only an upstream plugin.

cards.front: What does an inversion engine consume and emit?
cards.back: Consumes: Observations plus a model domain (a mesh over the Engineering Frame). Emits: a PropertyModel with uncertainty and provenance. The output re-enters the system as an ordinary property model.

cards.front: What two parts make up an inversion engine, and what types may NOT cross its boundary?
cards.back: A declarative `spec` (capabilities) plus a `run(ctx) → result` method. No SimPEG/PyGIMLi type ever crosses the boundary — the platform speaks only NumPy + its own Observation / PropertyModel / ModelDomain. Solver containers are built inside `run`, keeping libraries swappable and version-isolated.

cards.front: In what array order and frame does an engine return the recovered model?
cards.back: On the core cells as a Z-up `(z, y, x)` array — the same axis order and frame as everything else in storage.

cards.front: Why is the inversion mesh NOT the fused grid?
cards.back: Each method has its own optimal discretization; forcing all onto one grid would over-resolve cheap methods or under-resolve sharp ones. So inversion runs on a method-chosen mesh in the Engineering Frame and its result is resampled onto the fused grid afterward.

cards.front: What are the three mesh types and their uses?
cards.back: TensorMesh — regular rectilinear grid (uniform boxes); fast; gravity, magnetics, MT, simple DC. TreeMesh (octree) — adaptive, subdivides near sensors / coarsens at depth; EM and focused potential-field. SimplexMesh (tetrahedral) — unstructured, conforms to topography and electrodes; ERT/IP and seismic tomography over rough terrain.

cards.front: What are the Core region, Padding, and Active cells in a ModelDomain?
cards.back: Core — the target volume at full resolution; the only part trusted and resampled onto the fused grid. Padding — geometrically expanding cells pushing the solver boundary far from the target (fields are non-local); they never leave the engine. Active cells — topography-aware set excluding inactive air above the surface.

cards.front: Why is uncertainty mandatory for every inversion engine?
cards.back: Methods 'see' differently (gravity/MT smooth and non-unique; seismic sharp), and fusion and well planning are only honest if that survives. A model with no uncertainty is invalid — the result constructor raises an error if sigma is None.

cards.front: What is the tiered menu of uncertainty methods, and which is the default?
cards.back: Descending rigour: (A) posterior covariance / model-resolution matrix; (B) sensitivity-/DOI-weighted confidence; (C) ensemble spread; (D) flat registry prior, flagged low-confidence. Tier B is the expected default.

cards.front: How does the gravity engine derive σ (tier B)?
cards.back: From SimPEG's per-cell sensitivity weights: a low weight means a poorly-constrained (deep/edge) cell, which becomes a large σ. So uncertainty literally reflects where the physics can and can't resolve.

cards.front: Why is SimPEG linear gravity the first ('plumbing-proof') engine?
cards.back: Gravity's forward operator is linear, so it's fast and well-understood, wiring the whole phase together with the fewest moving parts. It builds a gravity.Survey and Simulation3DIntegral over active TensorMesh cells (unknown = density anomaly Δρ), runs L2 Tikhonov, then adds background density for an absolute model.

cards.front: Why is PyGIMLi ERT the first geothermally-meaningful engine, and what does it recover?
cards.back: Resistivity is a direct clue to hot conductive fluid. ERT consumes a dipole-dipole apparent-resistivity pseudosection + electrode geometry, runs a 2-D inversion on a topography-conforming SimplexMesh, and recovers a resistivity model with PyGIMLi's coverage (cumulative sensitivity) as tier-B uncertainty.

cards.front: Why is ERT swept across a thin y extent, and what does coverage-derived σ do off-section?
cards.back: ERT is intrinsically a line survey — the inversion lives in the vertical section under the electrode line — so the 2-D result is swept across the thin y extent of the core. The coverage-derived σ inflates off-section, properly distrusting cells the line can't see.

cards.front: Which inversion methods are workstation-feasible vs. need a server?
cards.back: Local-first: gravity, magnetics, ERT/IP, seismic refraction (traveltime). 'Bring a server': MT (marginal) and EM/tdem/fdem/aem (server/GPU). Each engine declares a compute profile and executionMode so the same engine can run local or remote — local-first ≠ local-only.

cards.front: What are the three coupling stages 5a, 5b, 5c?
cards.back: 5a — single-method (one method → one model, no coupling; build first). 5b — cooperative/sequential (invert A, feed it into B as reference/weight; a DAG of ordinary 5a jobs, no new solver math). 5c — joint/simultaneous (multiple methods on a shared mesh in one objective; roadmap, heaviest, not built this phase).

cards.front: How does cooperative (5b) inversion use a 'structure-guided weight'?
cards.back: Invert method A, then feed A's recovered model into B as a reference/starting model or a structure weight: high where A has sharp gradients → likely a geological boundary → relax B's smoothness across it. Computed by normalizing the gradient magnitude of A's model to [0,1].

cards.front: What are the two coupling mechanisms in joint (5c) inversion?
cards.back: Cross-gradient — penalize $\nabla m_A \times \nabla m_B$ so structures align without assuming a rock-physics law. Petrophysically coupled (PGI) — a rock-physics relationship ties the models together.

cards.front: What is the sequencing rule before starting joint (5c) inversion?
cards.back: Don't start 5c until ≥2 methods work standalone (5a) and cooperative coupling (5b) is validated against synthetic ground truth. 5b already delivers most of the multi-method payoff without a shared-mesh joint solver.

cards.front: Why must inversion engines be validated against synthetic ground truth?
cards.back: Because inversion is non-unique, you can't trust an engine until checked against a known answer. The synthetic generator forward-models a known earth into observations; you invert those and score the recovered model against the truth on the shared fused grid.

cards.front: What four checks/metrics validate an inversion engine?
cards.back: Data fit (misfit reaches χ ≈ 1, neither over- nor under-fit); Model recovery (RMSE, structural similarity, anomaly localisation & amplitude vs truth); Uncertainty honesty (truth falls inside stated confidence at expected rate — calibration); Resolution realism (recovered smoothness/DOI matches the method's physics).

cards.front: When is an inversion engine considered 'done'?
cards.back: When it recovers the synthetic anomaly within its physical resolution limits AND its uncertainty is calibrated.

cards.front: Why do inversions run as background jobs, and what does the harness do?
cards.back: They take minutes-to-hours and must survive restarts, so they run on the job queue. The harness conducts: validate params against paramsSchema before enqueueing (fail fast) → run the engine → supply a default tier-B uncertainty if none → persist the core as a PropertyModel → resample onto the fused grid.

cards.front: What is InversionProvenance and why does it matter?
cards.back: A record on every result of engine id/version, exact params (incl. applied defaults), mesh fingerprint, observation inputs, seed, and convergence diagnostics. Inversion results are interpretations, not measurements, so they must be fully auditable — answering 'what produced this?' and making 're-run with χ = 1.5' a one-field change.

cards.front: In one sentence, what is this simulator?
cards.back: A browser-based 3-D earth model builder that ingests every kind of subsurface survey, reconciles them into a single coordinate system and data model, fuses them onto a shared 3-D grid, transforms raw physics into quantities a geothermal engineer cares about, and renders an interactive volume you can slice, fly through, and plan a well into — always carrying uncertainty.

cards.front: What familiar backend-engineering shape does the simulator map onto?
cards.back: An ETL + rendering pipeline for the Earth: Extract (parse survey files) → Transform (normalize to one frame/units/schema) → Load/serve (chunked array storage streamed over HTTP) → Render (GPU volume viewer).

cards.front: In the ETL analogy, which steps are the hard, domain-specific part and why?
cards.back: The 'fuse' and 'derive' steps in the middle. They're hard because the inputs don't measure the same physical thing, aren't on the same grid, and disagree in ways that are physically correct — reconciling them is the product.

cards.front: Name the six layers of the architecture, bottom to top.
cards.back: 1) Spatial Framework, 2) Storage, 3) Ingestion, 4) Domain/Model, 5) Processing/Compute, 6) API/Serving — with the Client (browser) on top. Each layer depends only on those below it.

cards.front: Which layer is the 'kernel' of the architecture, and what does it contain?
cards.back: The Spatial Framework — the single CRS, vertical datum, and units registry that every number is expressed in. Nothing above it invents its own coordinates; it's the base types/units library every module imports.

cards.front: Why is the Spatial Framework (not graphics) deliberately the bottom layer?
cards.back: Because the deepest, most expensive bug in geoscience software is a coordinate mistake — wrong location, depth confused with elevation, feet mixed with metres. Forcing every number through one frame and one units registry at the bottom catches those bugs once, centrally, instead of in twelve different parsers.

cards.front: What does the Storage layer consist of?
cards.back: A catalog database (metadata, bounding boxes, provenance) plus a chunked array store for bulk 3-D/4-D data, plus a verbatim copy of every raw file. Analogy: a metadata DB + an object store of tiled binary blobs.

cards.front: What is the Ingestion layer, and why is it plugin-based?
cards.back: One adapter per survey format that parses a raw file and emits records fitting the data model. Adapters are plugins behind a common interface, so adding a new method needs no core changes.

cards.front: What are the three primitive record types every method maps onto?
cards.back: Observation (what was measured, where — raw and immutable), Property Model (a continuous 3-D field of one physical property), and Geological Feature (a discrete interpreted shape).

cards.front: Primitive: define 'Observation' and its CS analogy.
cards.back: What was measured, where — raw and immutable. CS analogy: an append-only event log; the source of truth you never mutate. Example: 400 gravity readings at GPS points, or an MT impedance tensor per station.

cards.front: Primitive: define 'Property Model' and its CS analogy.
cards.back: A continuous 3-D field of one physical property. CS analogy: a dense N-D array (tensor) with units and a coordinate support. Example: a resistivity cube from an inversion, or a temperature volume.

cards.front: Primitive: define 'Geological Feature' and its CS analogy.
cards.back: A discrete shape a human or algorithm interpreted. CS analogy: vector geometry — points, lines, meshes — with attributes. Example: a fault surface, a well path, or a microseismic event cloud.

cards.front: What is resistivity, its units, and its inverse?
cards.back: Resistivity is how strongly a rock opposes electrical current, in Ω·m. Its inverse is conductivity (S/m). It's the single most diagnostic property for geothermal work.

cards.front: How does rock type affect resistivity?
cards.back: Hot, salty, clay-rich rock conducts well (low resistivity); dry, cold, crystalline rock resists current (high resistivity).

cards.front: What is a fault, and why does it matter for geothermal?
cards.back: A fracture in rock where the two sides have moved relative to each other. Faults can be the plumbing that lets hot water rise.

cards.front: Why is a borehole/well special among survey methods?
cards.back: It's the only place rock is measured directly, rather than inferred from surface physics.

cards.front: What is the Engineering Frame?
cards.back: One local right-handed metre grid (X = East, Y = North, Z = Up) that every coordinate lives in. Real-world georeferencing is an optional rigid transform applied on top.

cards.front: What does 'Provenance' mean in this system, and how strict is it?
cards.back: Every artifact records where it came from: which raw files, which transforms, which versions. Lineage is mandatory — there is no record without provenance.

cards.front: What does carrying 'Uncertainty' through fusion accomplish?
cards.back: Every property model carries an error/resolution estimate that survives into fusion, so a smooth, vague survey is never silently trusted as much as a sharp one.

cards.front: List the six rungs of the progressive fusion ladder.
cards.back: 1) Visual co-registration & overlay, 2) Cross-plotting & statistics, 3) Rock-physics transforms, 4) Geological modeling, 5) Cooperative/joint inversion, 6) Drilling & well-path planning.

cards.front: Which rungs of the fusion ladder constitute the MVP?
cards.back: Rungs 1–3: visual overlay → cross-plotting → rock-physics transforms. Rungs 4–6 (geomodeling, joint inversion, well planning) are later phases.

cards.front: Fusion ladder rung 2 (cross-plotting) is what operation in CS terms?
cards.back: A JOIN on cell index across volumes — because everything is resampled onto one shared grid, you can sample several property volumes at the same points and correlate them (e.g. resistivity vs density).

cards.front: What do rock-physics transforms (rung 3) produce, with an example relation?
cards.back: They convert geophysical properties into geothermal targets via physics relations — e.g. Archie's law (resistivity + temperature → fluid likelihood) — producing derived volumes like a geothermal favorability field.

cards.front: Distinguish forward modeling from inversion in geophysics.
cards.back: Forward modeling: given an earth, predict what an instrument would measure (relatively easy — simulate the physics). Inversion: given the measurements, reconstruct the earth — an ill-posed, non-unique, computationally heavy inverse problem needing regularization/priors.

cards.front: What does the MVP do regarding inversion, and what does it NOT do?
cards.back: The MVP consumes the output of inversion (finished resistivity/velocity/density models) plus stores raw observations verbatim — it focuses on fusing, transforming, and visualizing. It does NOT run inversion itself; that's a pluggable later-phase module behind a stable interface.

cards.front: Why does inversion output not require any special-case code?
cards.back: Inversion output is just another PropertyModel, so it flows into storage, fusion, and the viewer through the exact same path as an ingested model. The day the plugin arrives, its output reuses all existing code.

cards.front: When does ingestion emit a PropertyModel vs an Observation?
cards.back: It emits a PropertyModel when a file is already inverted, and an Observation when it's raw. Raw-only surveys are still stored and visualizable as observations; they just wait for the inversion plugin.

cards.front: What is the synthetic data generator and why is keeping ground truth valuable?
cards.back: A separate Python package that builds a fully known fake planet, forward-models what each survey would measure, and writes those out in real industry file formats. Because the ground truth is known, every fusion result can be scored against a known answer. It feeds the normal ingestion path — just another data source.

cards.front: Why is Python the backend language choice?
cards.back: The geoscience ecosystem IS Python: pyproj (coordinates), xarray/Zarr (chunked arrays), rasterio, verde (gridding), segyio (seismic), lasio (well logs), ObsPy, and later SimPEG/PyGIMLi (inversion), GemPy (geomodeling). Re-implementing these would be a multi-year mistake. FastAPI adds async I/O and typed schemas.

cards.front: Why Three.js for 3-D rendering?
cards.back: The viewer's core need is GPU volume rendering — ray-marching a Data3DTexture in a custom shader with per-property transfer functions. Three.js exposes raw WebGL/WebGPU and 3-D textures, which higher-level globe libraries do not.

cards.front: Why Zarr v3 for bulk 3-D/4-D storage?
cards.back: Zarr is 'tiled image pyramids generalized to N-D and streamable over HTTP range requests.' A 64³ chunk maps directly to one GPU brick, and a power-of-two pyramid gives level-of-detail.

cards.front: What handles long compute jobs, and how does the client track them?
cards.back: RQ + Redis behind one job contract. Long compute (resampling, transforms, future inversions) runs as tracked background jobs; the client subscribes over WebSocket.

cards.front: What is a DEM, and what is the default source used here?
cards.back: A Digital Elevation Model — a raster grid of surface elevation (a heightmap of terrain). The default source is Copernicus GLO-30 (~30 m global). Terrain is draped so voxels and geography share one frame.

cards.front: Which file formats does the Extract step parse?
cards.back: A dozen messy scientific formats including SEG-Y, LAS, EDI, .stg, GeoTIFF, QuakeML, and GeoJSON.

cards.front: What rendering technique does the viewer use on the GPU?
cards.back: A WebGL/WebGPU volume renderer that ray-marches a 3-D texture (Data3DTexture) on the GPU, with per-property transfer functions.

cards.front: What problem does 'rock physics' solve in the geothermal fusion pipeline?
cards.back: Geophysics measures physical properties (resistivity, density, velocity), but drillers care about heat, fluid, and permeability. Rock physics is the bridge: a library of equations that turn what geophysics measures into what geothermal cares about.

cards.front: Why does a geophysical survey never directly measure temperature or permeability?
cards.back: A survey can only measure a physical property of the rock+fluid system (how it conducts electricity, how fast sound travels, its density) because those are what bend a field a surface sensor can read. Each method is good evidence for one property and blind to the others.

cards.front: In the CS analogy, rock physics is a ___ of a lossy encoding.
cards.back: A decoder. The subsurface 'compresses' the truth (temperature, fluid, permeability) into a lossy, noisy encoding (resistivity, velocity, density) via physics; rock physics decodes it back — approximately and assumption-laden, since the same resistivity can decode to different temperatures depending on porosity and salinity.

cards.front: Until calibrated, what is the status of every rock-physics output?
cards.back: Every output is a likelihood / proxy field, never a measured value — a rule the engine enforces mechanically. The relationships are site-calibratable approximations, not universal truths, and every parameter is first-class and user-tunable.

cards.front: What does Archie's law assume about the rock matrix, and where does conductivity come from?
cards.back: Archie's law assumes a clean (clay-free) rock whose matrix is an insulator; the entire bulk electrical conductivity comes from the conductive brine in the pore space.

cards.front: State Archie's law in conductivity form.
cards.back: σ_t = σ_w · φ^m · S_w^n / a, where σ_t is bulk conductivity, σ_w is pore-brine conductivity, φ is porosity, S_w is water saturation, m is cementation exponent, n is saturation exponent, and a is the tortuosity factor.

cards.front: In Archie's law, what are φ and S_w, and how do they differ?
cards.back: φ (porosity) is the fraction of total rock volume that is pore space (0–1). S_w (water saturation) is the fraction of that pore space filled with water (0–1). Both are dimensionless.

cards.front: What does the cementation exponent m represent in Archie's law, and its typical range?
cards.back: m describes how tortuous/connected the pores are, typically ≈1.3–2.5. (Saturation exponent n ≈1.5–2.5; tortuosity factor a ≈0.5–2.5, often taken as 1.)

cards.front: Define the formation factor F and resistivity index I from Archie's law.
cards.back: F = a/φ^m (formation factor) and I = S_w^(−n) (resistivity index). These are the classic packaging of Archie's law.

cards.front: Geothermally, low resistivity implies one of which three things?
cards.back: Low resistivity ⇒ either high porosity, high saturation, or hot/salty fluid. Archie's law is how you disentangle which of these is responsible.

cards.front: Archie solved for water saturation S_w (clay-free).
cards.back: S_w = (a·ρ_w / (φ^m · ρ_t))^(1/n), where ρ_w is brine resistivity and ρ_t is measured true resistivity. This is the archie_saturation transform.

cards.front: Why does the Archie saturation transform recommend Monte-Carlo uncertainty mode?
cards.back: Because φ enters as φ^m, Archie is strongly nonlinear in porosity, so a small porosity error blows up in S_w. The linearized delta-method default is inadequate, so Monte-Carlo propagation is recommended.

cards.front: What physical fact underlies the Arps relation (resistivity → temperature)?
cards.back: Brine conducts better when hotter because ions move faster — roughly 2% per °C at fixed salinity. This makes electrical conductivity into a thermometer.

cards.front: What are the two steps of the resistivity_to_temperature.arps transform?
cards.back: Step 1: use Archie (a=1, full saturation) to back out pore-fluid conductivity: σ_w = σ_bulk / φ^m. Step 2: invert the Arps temperature line to get absolute temperature T in kelvin.

cards.front: Write the inverted Arps relation solving for temperature T.
cards.back: T = T_ref + (σ_w/σ_w(T_ref) − 1)/α, where α is the Arps slope (≈0.02 per K) and T_ref is the reference temperature (default 298.15 K = 25 °C). Output is in kelvin.

cards.front: What is the Arps slope α and its default value?
cards.back: α is the Arps slope ≈ 0.02 per K (the '2%/°C'), the fractional rise in brine conductivity per kelvin. Default param arps_slope_per_K = 0.02.

cards.front: Why is the Arps temperature output retitled 'temperature likelihood'?
cards.back: The class is calibration_status = 'uncalibrated', so the harness retitles the layer 'temperature likelihood' and stamps tier = 'proxy'. It cannot present as a measured temperature until a calibration run promotes it — and even then only near the wells. This is the scientific-honesty gate.

cards.front: Why is seismic velocity a porosity proxy?
cards.back: Sound travels fast through solid rock and slow through fluid-filled pores, so higher porosity lowers V_p. Velocity-to-porosity transforms exploit this.

cards.front: State the Wyllie time-average equation and what it models.
cards.back: 1/V_p = (1−φ)/V_matrix + φ/V_fluid — total travel time is the volume-weighted sum of time through matrix and through fluid. Solving: φ = (1/V_p − 1/V_matrix)/(1/V_fluid − 1/V_matrix).

cards.front: State the Raymer-Hunt-Gardner (RHG) velocity-porosity equation and its advantage.
cards.back: V_p = (1−φ)²·V_matrix + φ·V_fluid. RHG is empirically more accurate than Wyllie at low porosity; it's solved via its quadratic.

cards.front: Default matrix and fluid velocities for velocity→porosity transforms?
cards.back: V_matrix defaults to 5500 m/s (solid mineral frame), V_fluid defaults to 1500 m/s (brine). Output porosity is clamped to [0, 0.5].

cards.front: How does the density→porosity transform work, and its defaults?
cards.back: Simple mass balance: φ = (ρ_matrix − ρ_b)/(ρ_matrix − ρ_fluid), with ρ_matrix defaulting to 2650 kg/m³ (quartz) and ρ_fluid to 1000 kg/m³ (brine).

cards.front: Why does plain Archie over-read water in shaly/altered rock, and what fixes it?
cards.back: Clay conducts on its own (charged mineral surfaces hold mobile ions), so Archie mistakes clay conduction for brine conduction. The Waxman-Smits model adds a clay term in parallel with the brine path to correct this.

cards.front: State the Waxman-Smits conductivity equation.
cards.back: 1/ρ_t = φ^m·S_w^n/(a·ρ_w) + B·Q_v·S_w^(n−1), where B is equivalent counter-ion conductance (≈4.6 S/m per meq/mL at 25 °C) and Q_v is cation-exchange capacity per pore volume (∝ clay volume).

cards.front: How is Waxman-Smits solved, given no closed form for S_w?
cards.back: There is no closed form, so the code solves S_w by a handful of fixed-point iterations. DualWaterSaturation subclasses it with the same solver but a different spec (bound vs free pore water).

cards.front: Why is the clay correction crucial in geothermal systems specifically?
cards.back: Hydrothermal systems are full of conductive alteration clay, so the clay correction is often the difference between 'there's fluid here' and 'that's just the clay cap.'

cards.front: What is the hydrothermal 'clay cap' and how does the alteration_index detect it?
cards.back: Hydrothermal alteration produces conductive smectite clay forming a low-resistivity cap over reservoirs. alteration_index is a heuristic: a smooth low-resistivity membership L = σ((log₁₀ρ_thresh − log₁₀ρ)/w), where σ is the logistic function and w a log-width.

cards.front: How does gmm_alteration_posterior differ from the heuristic alteration_index?
cards.back: It is data-driven: it fits a 2-component Gaussian mixture to log₁₀ρ and returns the posterior probability of the low-resistivity class — 'let the data find the threshold' instead of a fixed one. It's clustering-as-a-transform.

cards.front: Why is permeability the hardest of the three geothermal ingredients to see remotely?
cards.back: Permeability is the existence of connected cracks for fluid to flow through; it cannot be measured directly remotely. It's inferred via proxies like microseismic fracture density and the V_p/V_s ratio.

cards.front: How does the microseismic_density transform estimate fracture density?
cards.back: Microseismic events cluster where rock is actively fracturing. The transform takes the event cloud binned to per-cell counts, applies a 3-D Gaussian kernel density estimate (KDE), and peak-normalizes to a [0,1] fracture-density index. Bandwidth (kernel σ) is the tunable smoothing knob.

cards.front: How does vp_vs_fracture_proxy flag fractured zones?
cards.back: Open or fluid-filled fractures lower the V_p/V_s ratio, so a low-V_p/V_s membership flags fractured zones.

cards.front: How does fracture_to_permeability map fracture index to permeability k?
cards.back: By log-linear interpolation between a tight-matrix floor and well-fractured ceiling: log₁₀k = log₁₀k_min + d_frac·(log₁₀k_max − log₁₀k_min), then damped by alteration: k ← k·(1 − s·alteration), since clay gouge seals fractures.

cards.front: Defaults for k_min, k_max, and the alteration-sealing factor s in fracture_to_permeability?
cards.back: k_min = 1e-17 m² (tight-matrix floor, ≈0.01 mD), k_max = 1e-12 m² (well-fractured ceiling, ≈1 darcy), s = 0.5 (alteration-sealing factor). Its first assumption declares it a low-confidence heuristic, not a flow/percolation simulation.

cards.front: What two parts make up the transform-engine design, and what does each do?
cards.back: A declarative spec (class attributes: id, version, title, target, inputs, output, params, assumptions, calibration_status) makes a transform discoverable, parameterizable, versionable, UI-drivable — the typed interface. Plus a pure function apply() that does the physics.

cards.front: What is the contract of a transform's apply() function?
cards.back: It receives each input as a unit-converted NumPy array and each param as a scalar, and returns the output array. No I/O, no units, no masking, no storage — those are the harness's job. Pure in, pure out, trivially testable.

cards.front: What are the eight steps run_transform() guarantees? (name the key ones)
cards.back: 1) Resolve inputs to the fused grid, 2) unit-check & convert via pint, 3) build valid mask (AND of required inputs' coverage → NaN where missing), 4) vectorized apply on valid cells, 5) clamp to valid_range, 6) propagate σ, 7) stamp calibration honesty, 8) write output + σ with provenance.

cards.front: What happens to a cell missing any required input, and why?
cards.back: It becomes NaN (nodata), never silently zero-filled — the footprint-honesty rule. This is why a derived field has holes exactly where its inputs don't overlap. A wrong-dimension input is a hard error.

cards.front: How is the output tier of a derived field determined?
cards.back: The output tier is the minimum tier over the inputs, then capped by the transform's own calibration status — an uncalibrated transform can never emit better than 'proxy'.

cards.front: What makes a transform's output indistinguishable from an ingested property model, and what's the one difference?
cards.back: It's a first-class PropertyModel: same data-model schema, same Zarr storage, same renderer. The only difference is a provenance/derivation block — a complete, reproducible recipe (transformId, version, inputs, params, calibrationStatus, tier, assumptions).

cards.front: Why can rock-physics transforms be chained, and what does re-running with new params produce?
cards.back: Because each output IS a PropertyModel, you can chain (e.g. velocity→porosity, then porosity+resistivity→saturation). Since the derivation block is a complete recipe, any derived volume is fully reproducible; re-running with different params yields a new versioned instance, never an in-place mutation.

cards.front: What is the default uncertainty-propagation method, and its formula?
cards.back: The delta method (first-order error propagation): σ_y² ≈ Σ_i (∂f/∂x_i)²σ_x_i² + Σ_θ (∂f/∂θ)²σ_θ². Partial derivatives are computed numerically so authors never hand-derive a Jacobian.

cards.front: What is the second sum in the delta-method formula, and when can it dominate?
cards.back: It's parameter uncertainty: declare a sigma on a Param (e.g. porosity σ=0.03) and it contributes to output uncertainty. It often dominates once a field is calibrated. For strongly nonlinear transforms (Archie), opt into Monte-Carlo mode instead.

cards.front: What is geothermal 'favorability'?
cards.back: The special transform that fuses all evidence layers into one targetable index in [0,1] — the 'drill here' index a driller actually points at. It's the headline product of the rock-physics stage.

cards.front: What is the headline geothermal idea behind favorability?
cards.back: A producible geothermal play needs heat AND fluid AND permeability in the same cell. Heat with no fluid is dry hot rock; fluid with no permeability is trapped. Each survey method sees only one of the three, so favorability is where they line up.

cards.front: Why is a weighted linear sum 'catastrophically wrong' as the default favorability rule?
cards.back: It is compensatory: a soaring temperature score can numerically offset absent permeability, painting a dry-hot cell as 'favorable.' That's the worst failure mode — it sends a drill rig to a place with no fluid path.

cards.front: What operator does the default favorability use to encode 'AND', and why?
cards.back: Fuzzy-conjunction (non-compensatory): F_AND = min_i(e_i) (Zadeh AND) or ∏_i e_i (product AND), so any absent required layer pulls the cell toward 0. Geothermal favorability is a weakest-link problem, so the operator must be unforgiving (min/product) not forgiving (mean).

cards.front: How does supporting evidence combine with required evidence in favorability?
cards.back: Supporting evidence can only boost via a soft fuzzy-OR (max), never rescue a missing required conjunct: F = F_AND + (1 − F_AND)·F_OR, where F_OR = max over supporting layers.

cards.front: Name the three favorability combination methods and their status.
cards.back: fuzzy (default, non-compensatory weakest-link, physically faithful); weighted (exploratory, compensatory, with a missing-required guard that flags/excludes such cells from top-targets); bayesian (weights-of-evidence posterior odds, deferred — raises NotImplementedError until training data exists).

cards.front: In the NaN-aware _fuzzy_and code, what happens to a missing required conjunct?
cards.back: It is replaced with 0.0 (np.where(isfinite, stack, 0.0)), which pulls the product/min toward 0 — encoding that a missing required layer kills favorability for that cell.

cards.front: What is a CRS (Coordinate Reference System)?
cards.back: A precise, named recipe for turning a position on Earth into numbers and back. It bundles a model of the Earth's shape (datum/ellipsoid) with a coordinate system (angles or metres on a flat grid). Every CRS has an EPSG code identifying exactly how to interpret the numbers.

cards.front: What is an EPSG code, and why does a coordinate pair without a CRS matter?
cards.back: An EPSG code is a stable integer ID from a public registry (e.g. EPSG:4326) that acts like a well-known type ID specifying exactly how to interpret coordinates. A pair of numbers with no CRS is like a byte buffer with no charset — meaningless and dangerous.

cards.front: Name the four spatial schemes you'll meet and what their numbers represent.
cards.back: Geographic (lat/lon, angles in degrees, e.g. EPSG:4326); Projected (UTM/national grids, metres on a flat map, e.g. EPSG:32612); Web Mercator (metres-ish for web tiles, EPSG:3857); Local engineering grid (metres from a chosen local origin).

cards.front: Why is Web Mercator (EPSG:3857) banned for measurement?
cards.back: It is conformal but wildly area- and distance-distorting (a km near the poles measures very differently than at the equator); it exists to make square map tiles line up, not to measure. It's allowed only for draping satellite imagery on the surface at render time, isolated in the viewer.

cards.front: What is UTM and why is it the platform's default projected CRS?
cards.back: Universal Transverse Mercator chops the world into 60 north–south zones each 6° wide, accepting tiny bounded distortion within each zone. For a typical 1–10 km geothermal site that distortion is negligible, so UTM is auto-selected as the default projected CRS.

cards.front: Why is map projection inherently lossy?
cards.back: Like any map of a sphere on flat paper, you cannot preserve angles, areas, and distances all at once — projecting the curved Earth into flat metres always sacrifices something.

cards.front: Define the Engineering Frame.
cards.back: A local, right-handed Cartesian frame with ENU axes (X=East, Y=North, Z=Up), in metres, origin at a project-chosen anchor. Z is elevation, positive up. It is the single internal coordinate system in which everything resamples, renders, and fuses.

cards.front: In the Engineering Frame architecture, what is georeferencing reduced to?
cards.back: Just an optional rigid transform from the Engineering Frame out to a real CRS. Georeferenced and local modes share one code path; geo-anchoring matters only at three boundaries: ingest, terrain/basemap loading, and export.

cards.front: How do georeferenced mode and local mode differ?
cards.back: Georeferenced mode has a real projected CRS, a real anchor (E,N,elev), and real DEM/map tiles. Local mode has no CRS, origin at (0,0,0), and a flat/synthetic surface. Everything else is identical, so synthetic and real data fuse through the same code.

cards.front: Why does the platform use a local frame instead of raw UTM coordinates? (float32 jitter)
cards.back: The GPU renders in float32 (~7 significant digits). Large UTM coordinates make the spacing between representable values coarse, causing visible sub-metre wobble/shimmer. A local frame keeps numbers small for high precision — the classic 'large world coordinates' bug.

cards.front: Estimate float32 quantization at a UTM easting of ~412,300 m.
cards.back: ε ≈ 2^(⌊log₂(412300)⌋−23) = 2^(18−23) = 2^−5 ≈ 0.03 m per axis, just from the coordinate's magnitude before any math.

cards.front: What precision does the floating-origin pattern buy at a ±5 km site?
cards.back: Subtracting a local origin keeps coordinates small. At x ≈ ±50,000 m, ε ≈ 2^(15−23) = 2^−8 ≈ 4 mm; for a typical ±5 km site you reach sub-millimetre precision — same data, same GPU, just smaller numbers.

cards.front: What is the SpatialFrame object and how much data is it?
cards.back: Each project owns exactly one SpatialFrame — small metadata (a catalog row), not bulk data. It holds mode, horizontal_crs, vertical_datum, anchor, rotation_deg, ENU axis convention, length_unit, roi, depth_range, surface_model, and georef_status.

cards.front: What is the Engineering→CRS transform formula?
cards.back: A 2-D rotation by θ (rotation_deg) plus anchor translation, elevation passed through: easting = anchor.E + (x·cosθ − y·sinθ); northing = anchor.N + (x·sinθ + y·cosθ); elev = anchor.elevation + z. In local mode this is the identity.

cards.front: How does the platform auto-select the horizontal CRS in georeferenced mode?
cards.back: Take the ROI centroid, then auto-select the UTM zone containing it (326## north / 327## south). At 1–10 km extent distortion is negligible and it's a standard EPSG every external tool understands. The anchor defaults to the ROI centroid at surface elevation.

cards.front: What CRS escalations are surfaced to the user instead of handled silently?
cards.back: ROI straddling a UTM zone boundary or basin/regional scale (≳50 km) → custom Transverse Mercator centred on the ROI (inline WKT2); high latitude (>84°N / <80°S) → polar stereographic; antimeridian crossing → custom TM centred on ROI.

cards.front: What's the difference between `mode` and `georef_status`?
cards.back: `mode` only says whether a transform to a real CRS exists. `georef_status` is a separate quality flag saying whether that transform is trustworthy — the two are independent.

cards.front: List the georef_status levels from least to most trustworthy.
cards.back: unknown (no spatial info); assumed_local (Engineering by fiat, not a real place); anchored (transform assigned, not checked); validated (anchor checked against control within tolerance); survey_controlled (surveyed GNSS/ground-control — authoritative).

cards.front: Why does re-anchoring a local dataset NOT make it 'real'?
cards.back: Because bulk arrays are always stored in Engineering coords, georeference() just sets metadata with zero array reprocessing. But the bytes being unchanged doesn't mean the data is physically correct — anchoring sets status to 'anchored', never 'validated'.

cards.front: What is the canonical vertical coordinate internally?
cards.back: Orthometric elevation — height above the project's vertical datum (default EGM2008 geoid / mean sea level), in metres, positive up. Everything else (depth, TVD, TVDSS, MD) is a derived view computed on demand, never the source of truth.

cards.front: Orthometric vs ellipsoidal height — what's the difference?
cards.back: Orthometric height is height above the geoid (the bumpy gravitational sea-level surface; what levels and DEMs report). Ellipsoidal height is height above the smooth mathematical ellipsoid (what raw GPS reports). They differ by the geoid separation, tens of metres.

cards.front: What is the geoid?
cards.back: Because Earth's gravity field is lumpy, 'sea level' isn't a smooth ellipsoid but a bumpy equipotential surface called the geoid. Orthometric height is measured above it.

cards.front: Convert between elevation and depth-from-surface.
cards.back: depth = surface_elev − z, and z = surface_elev − depth, where z is canonical elevation and surface_elev(x,y) is ground elevation at that map position. (elevation_to_depth / depth_to_elevation in vertical.py)

cards.front: What is TVDSS and how is it computed from elevation?
cards.back: TVDSS (true vertical depth subsea) is metres below MSL/datum: TVDSS = −z (elevation_to_tvdss). It differs from depth-from-surface, which subtracts from local ground elevation rather than the datum.

cards.front: Why can TVDSS and depth-from-surface differ greatly for the same rock?
cards.back: A well on a 1620 m plateau hitting z = −380 m has TVDSS = −z = 380 m (below datum) but depth-from-surface = 1620 − (−380) = 2000 m drilled. Same rock, two correct depths differing by the surface elevation (1620 m). Storing canonical elevation and deriving each depth makes confusing them impossible.

cards.front: Define MD vs TVD.
cards.back: MD (measured depth) is length along the borehole; TVD (true vertical depth) is straight-down depth from a well reference. MD needs the well's deviation survey; TVD is obtained by integrating that survey. They're equal only for a perfectly vertical well.

cards.front: What are KB, GL, and MSL as well reference data?
cards.back: KB = kelly bushing, a point on the rig floor a few metres above ground; GL = ground level; MSL = mean sea level. A log reading '1000 m' means 1000 m below that well's reference, so each well stores its reference kind and elevation.

cards.front: What is a deviation survey?
cards.back: A table of down-hole stations, each giving (MD, inclination, azimuth): measured depth, angle off vertical (0°=straight down, 90°=horizontal), and compass bearing CW from North. It's a sparse sampled signal of the trajectory; the full 3-D path is reconstructed by integration.

cards.front: What is the minimum curvature method?
cards.back: The standard integration method for reconstructing a borehole path: between two adjacent stations it assumes the hole follows a circular arc (smoothest curve through both directions) rather than two straight tangents. Implemented in min_curvature_positions, shared by ingested wells and the well planner.

cards.front: In minimum curvature, what is the dogleg angle β and the ratio factor RF?
cards.back: β is the dogleg angle — total change in direction over the interval (angle between the two unit direction vectors), via cosβ = cos(I₂−I₁) − sinI₁·sinI₂·(1−cos(A₂−A₁)). RF = (2/β)·tan(β/2) corrects the straight-segment average to follow the arc; RF→1 as β→0 (code guards β<1e−7).

cards.front: What are the minimum-curvature position increments ΔN, ΔE, ΔV?
cards.back: ΔN = (ΔMD/2)(sinI₁cosA₁ + sinI₂cosA₂)·RF; ΔE = (ΔMD/2)(sinI₁sinA₁ + sinI₂sinA₂)·RF; ΔV = (ΔMD/2)(cosI₁ + cosI₂)·RF. ΔV is downward, so code does z = z_prev − ΔV (Z is up). TVD accumulates ΔV.

cards.front: What is dogleg severity (DLS) and why does it matter?
cards.back: DLS = β·(30/ΔMD) — degrees of bend per 30 m, the industry standard for how sharply a hole is turning. It feeds directly into well-planning drillability checks.

cards.front: What is the units registry's core rule and how does it work?
cards.back: Every numeric quantity carries an explicit unit; nothing is dimensionless by assumption. A single pint registry converts to canonical internal units on ingest, storing the canonical unit in metadata and the original in provenance. to_canonical() parses external units in; to_display() formats at the UI edge.

cards.front: List several canonical internal units (length, resistivity, density, seismic velocity, gravity, magnetic field).
cards.back: length/coordinates = m; resistivity = Ω·m; conductivity = S/m; density = kg/m³; seismic velocity = m/s; gravity anomaly = mGal; magnetic field = nT; InSAR deformation = mm. (mGal = 1e−3 Gal where Gal = cm/s².)

cards.front: Why is temperature stored canonically in kelvin, not °C?
cards.back: pint treats degC as an offset unit: an absolute temperature and a temperature difference share the symbol but aren't the same quantity (20°C absolute = 293.15 K, but a 20°C change = 20 K). Storing a stddev, gradient, or difference as degC silently corrupts arithmetic by adding the 273.15 offset. Kelvin avoids this; °C is for display only.

cards.front: What are the canonical units for temperature, its gradient, and its uncertainty?
cards.back: Absolute temperature → K (key `temperature`); temperature gradient → K/km (`temperature_gradient`); temperature uncertainty/Δ → K (`temperature_sigma`). Convert to °C only for display. A temperature field's _sigma array is in K, not °C.

cards.front: Why is 'chargeability' stored as three distinct property types, not one unit?
cards.back: Time-domain IP, frequency-domain IP, and IP phase are distinct physical measurements with incompatible units (chargeability_time_ms in ms; chargeability_mv_v in mV/V; IP phase in mrad). Collapsing them into one canonical unit would be a category error, like storing duration and frequency in one column.

cards.front: What is induced polarization (IP)?
cards.back: IP measures how much the ground temporarily stores charge (like a leaky capacitor) — a proxy for clay and disseminated sulphide minerals.

cards.front: What does the property-type registry pin per property?
cards.back: Per property it pins canonical unit, default colourmap, log/linear scaling, a sensible display range, and the interpolation space used by fusion (e.g. resistivity interpolates in log₁₀ because it spans orders of magnitude). Declaring a property once makes the whole stack handle it; plugins register new keys here.

cards.front: Why are boreholes special compared to every other survey method?
cards.back: A borehole is the only place we touch the rock directly — instruments lowered down the hole read properties at the rock itself. This makes wells the 'ground truth' / calibration anchor that tells remote inference methods whether their guesses are right.

cards.front: What is the fundamental trade-off of a well log vs. remote surveys?
cards.back: A well is 1D: infinite (near-perfect, centimetre-scale) resolution but zero coverage — a single thread through a 3D earth. Remote methods are the opposite: full-volume coverage but lossy and low-resolution.

cards.front: In ML terms, what role does a well play relative to remote surveys?
cards.back: Remote surveys are a model predicting rock properties over a 3D grid; a well is a column of ground-truth labels. You can't label the whole volume (drilling costs millions per hole), but the few labels you have calibrate and validate the model.

cards.front: What does a logging tool / wireline do?
cards.back: A logging tool is a sensor lowered down the borehole on a cable (wireline). As it moves it records a continuous curve of one property versus depth — each curve is a signal sampled along the depth axis.

cards.front: What does the resistivity (RES) curve measure and why does it matter for geothermal?
cards.back: Electrical resistivity of the rock (Ω·m), the inverse of conductivity. Low resistivity = hot, salty, porous, clay-rich → the geothermal signature. It directly ties remote ERT/MT to truth.

cards.front: What does the gamma-ray (GR) curve measure and indicate?
cards.back: Natural radioactivity in API units. Shales/clays are radioactive; clean sands/carbonates are not — so GR is a clay/alteration indicator. It has no canonical property key, so it is carried as method-specific metadata.

cards.front: What does the density (DEN/RHOB) curve measure and what does it tie to?
cards.back: Bulk density (kg/m³). It ties to gravity surveys; porosity reduces density.

cards.front: What does the sonic (VP/DT) curve measure and what does it tie to?
cards.back: P-wave velocity Vp (m/s), often recorded as slowness Δt in µs/ft. It ties to seismic; soft/fractured/fluid-filled rock is slow. Vp is the speed of a compressional sound wave — faster in hard, dense, unfractured rock.

cards.front: What does the temperature (TEMP) curve give you?
cards.back: Rock/fluid temperature — 'the geothermal prize.' From it you derive the geothermal gradient and heat-flow.

cards.front: How do log curve mnemonics map to canonical property types?
cards.back: RES → resistivity, DEN/RHOB → density, VP/DT → velocity_p, TEMP → temperature. Gamma-ray (GR) has no canonical key and is carried as method-specific metadata.

cards.front: Why are wells the calibration anchor for rock physics?
cards.back: Rock physics equations convert geophysical properties (resistivity, Vp) into engineering targets (temperature, porosity, fluid), and have free parameters. A well gives both the geophysical property AND the geothermal answer in the SAME rock, so you can fit those parameters to reality instead of textbook guesses.

cards.front: MD vs. TVD: define each.
cards.back: MD (Measured Depth) = length of cable spooled out, i.e. distance ALONG the hole — the only thing the tool knows. TVD (True Vertical Depth) = straight-line vertical depth below the wellhead — what matters for reservoir depth and 3D placement.

cards.front: A well drills 2000 m of hole at 60° from vertical. What is its TVD?
cards.back: TVD = 2000 × cos 60° = 1000 m. Plotting the log at '2000 m depth' would place it 1000 m too deep and in the wrong horizontal spot.

cards.front: Why can't you convert MD to TVD/XYZ without extra data?
cards.back: MD is only the measurement axis; the geometry (TVD/XYZ) depends on the well's orientation along its path. You need the deviation survey to bridge them — MD ≠ TVD for any deviated well.

cards.front: What three columns make up a deviation survey, and their units?
cards.back: MD = measured depth at the station (m); INC (inclination) = angle from vertical, 0°=straight down / 90°=horizontal (degrees); AZI (azimuth) = compass heading, 0°=North / 90°=East (degrees).

cards.front: What assumption does minimum-curvature integration make about the well path?
cards.back: It assumes the hole follows a circular arc between two survey stations (rather than two straight segments) — a realistic, smooth path. It is the industry-standard trajectory integrator.

cards.front: In minimum curvature, what is the dogleg angle β?
cards.back: β is the total change in direction across an interval (in radians); a bigger β means a sharper bend. It is computed from the two stations' inclinations and azimuths: cos β = cos(I₂−I₁) − sin I₁ sin I₂ (1 − cos(A₂−A₁)).

cards.front: In minimum curvature, what is the ratio factor RF?
cards.back: RF = (2/β)·tan(β/2) is the correction that turns the straight chord into the arc length. As the bend vanishes (β→0), RF→1 and the formula reduces to a simple average of directions.

cards.front: What are the minimum-curvature step formulas for ΔE, ΔN, ΔV?
cards.back: ΔE = (ΔMD/2)(sin I₁ sin A₁ + sin I₂ sin A₂)·RF; ΔN = (ΔMD/2)(sin I₁ cos A₁ + sin I₂ cos A₂)·RF; ΔV = (ΔMD/2)(cos I₁ + cos I₂)·RF. ΔE/ΔN add to x/y; ΔV is the TVD increment.

cards.front: In a Z-up Engineering Frame, how is the vertical increment ΔV applied?
cards.back: ΔV is the TVD increment (vertical drop), so it is SUBTRACTED from elevation: z −= ΔV. ΔE and ΔN are added to x and y.

cards.front: What is dogleg severity (DLS) and its formula?
cards.back: DLS is the dogleg angle normalised to a standard length: DLS = β·(30/ΔMD), in degrees per 30 m. It's the well's turning rate, used by the well planner as a drillability limit.

cards.front: What happens during ingestion if an old LAS file has no deviation survey?
cards.back: The adapter emits a 'vertical-well assumption' warning and treats MD = TVD straight down from the wellhead — explicit and flagged, never a silent guess. The wellhead position (x, y, elevation) is still mandatory to place the well.

cards.front: What is bottom-hole temperature (BHT) and what's its main caveat?
cards.back: BHT is the temperature at the deepest point — the headline 'how hot does it get' number. Caveat: drilling cools the rock near the hole, so raw BHT readings are usually too low and need a correction.

cards.front: How is the geothermal gradient computed from a temperature log, and its units?
cards.back: It's the slope of temperature with depth: ∇T = dT/dz ≈ (T_bottom − T_surface)/depth, measured in K/km (canonical), often quoted in °C/km.

cards.front: What is a 'normal' continental geothermal gradient vs. a geothermal play?
cards.back: A normal continental gradient is ~25–30 °C/km. A Basin-&-Range geothermal play (the flagship scenario) runs ~45 °C/km — that elevated gradient is the first-order signal that there's exploitable heat.

cards.front: What is the surface heat-flow formula and its variables/units?
cards.back: q = k·∇T, where q is heat-flow (mW/m²), k is the rock's thermal conductivity (W/m·K), and ∇T is the geothermal gradient (K/m). High regional heat-flow is the classic 'drill here' indicator.

cards.front: Why does the platform store temperature in kelvin, not °C?
cards.back: The pint units library treats °C as an offset unit, so a temperature and a temperature difference become indistinguishable ('is 5 °C a temperature or a 5-degree change?'). Kelvin has no offset, so ΔT and T behave consistently. Files write °C; ingestion canonicalises to kelvin.

cards.front: What is the LAS (Log ASCII Standard) format structure?
cards.back: A universal text format for well logs: several header sections (each beginning with ~, e.g. ~VERSION, ~WELL, ~CURVE, ~PARAMETER) describing the well and curves, followed by a ~ASCII block of columnar data — one row per depth sample, read like a CSV with no commas.

cards.front: In a LAS ~WELL section, what do STRT, STOP, STEP, and NULL specify?
cards.back: STRT = first index value (start depth), STOP = last index value (stop depth), STEP = sample spacing (e.g. 0.5 m, the tool resolution), NULL = sentinel value for missing samples (e.g. −999.25).

cards.front: What is the join key linking a LAS file to its deviation survey CSV?
cards.back: The deviation survey ships as <well>_deviation.csv alongside the LAS and is joined by the file stem / well id (wellId, e.g. 'GT-1'). The adapter finds the sibling file automatically and feeds it to min_curvature_positions.

cards.front: Why do MD and DEPT(TVD) columns diverge as depth increases in a LAS data block?
cards.back: Because the well is deviated — as the hole bends away from vertical, the measured depth along the hole exceeds the true vertical depth. The numeric divergence is the deviation showing up in the data.

cards.front: Into which two normalized primitives does a LAS file split, and why?
cards.back: (1) The curves vs MD → an immutable ObservationSet (geometryKind 'wellcurve') — the sacred measured record, never edited. (2) The borehole path → a GeologicalFeature (featureKind 'wellPath') — a re-editable interpreted trajectory carrying the deviation survey. They're linked by wellId.

cards.front: Why keep the well curve (Observation) separate from the well path (Feature)?
cards.back: The measurement (curve) is sacred and immutable, while the geometry (path) can be re-computed if a better deviation survey arrives. There is deliberately no 'well_path' support kind — the path is a Feature, not a PropertyModel.

cards.front: How is each curve sample placed at its true 3D position?
cards.back: By interpolating its MD onto the minimum-curvature trajectory, so e.g. RES at MD 1732 m sits at the correct (x,y,z) — possibly hundreds of metres east of the wellhead. The 3D viewer then drapes each curve as a colour-mapped tube along the trajectory.

cards.front: How do continuous temperature logs vs. sparse BHT/spring points ingest?
cards.back: A continuous temperature log ingests as a wellcurve Observation (like other curves). Sparse bottom-hole-temperature or spring points ingest as point temperature Observations in kelvin, via the 'heatflow' method.

cards.front: What is electrical resistivity ρ, and what are its units?
cards.back: Resistivity ρ (rho) measures how strongly a material opposes electric current, in ohm-metres (Ω·m). It is the inverse of conductivity σ, so σ = 1/ρ.

cards.front: Why are electrical methods (ERT/IP) especially valuable for geothermal exploration?
cards.back: Resistivity drops dramatically in hot, salty water and clay. The conductive clay cap that ERT sees best is exactly the seal that traps a geothermal reservoir, so mapping resistivity maps the cap.

cards.front: How does resistivity differ between dry crystalline rock, brine-saturated porous rock, and clay?
cards.back: Dry, cold crystalline rock is very resistive (hundreds–thousands of Ω·m); porous rock saturated with saline fluid is conductive (ions carry current); clay (especially hydrothermal alteration clay) is very conductive (surfaces hold mobile ions).

cards.front: What does a low-resistivity blob underground indicate?
cards.back: Fluid, or clay, or both. In a geothermal system the conductive, clay-rich cap above a reservoir produces such a low-resistivity zone.

cards.front: State Archie's law and what it links.
cards.back: ρ_rock = a·φ^(−m)·S_w^(−n)·ρ_w. It empirically links bulk rock resistivity to porosity φ, water saturation S_w, and pore-fluid resistivity ρ_w (valid for clay-free, fluid-filled rock).

cards.front: In Archie's law, what do the exponents m and n represent (and typical values)?
cards.back: m is the cementation exponent (≈1.3–2.5, how connected the pores are); n is the saturation exponent (≈2). The constant a is tortuosity (≈0.6–1).

cards.front: According to Archie's law, what three changes push rock resistivity down?
cards.back: More porosity, more water saturation, and saltier/hotter (lower-ρ_w) pore fluid all lower bulk resistivity — the bridge from a geophysical number to geothermal targets like fluid and permeability.

cards.front: When does Archie's law break down, and what method compensates?
cards.back: It breaks down when clay is present, because clay conducts through its own surface chemistry, not just pore fluid. IP (induced polarization) is run to tell clay and brine apart.

cards.front: What four electrodes does ERT use, and what is each pair's role?
cards.back: Two current electrodes A and B push known current I into the ground; two potential electrodes M and N measure the resulting voltage difference ΔV. The set of four is called a quadrupole.

cards.front: What is the ERT apparent-resistivity formula?
cards.back: ρ_a = K·(ΔV/I), where K is a geometric factor depending only on the four electrode positions. (Ohm's law corrected for 3-D geometry.)

cards.front: What does the geometric factor K depend on?
cards.back: Only on where the four electrodes (A, B, M, N) sit — their geometry — not on the ground itself.

cards.front: Why is ERT described as a CT-scan / inverse problem?
cards.back: Each reading is a single line-integral-like probe through the ground, like one X-ray angle. No single reading is an image; the image emerges only after combining many readings and inverting them — ERT is electrical tomography.

cards.front: Why is the measured resistivity called 'apparent' resistivity?
cards.back: ρ_a is the resistivity the ground would have if it were uniform. Real ground isn't uniform, so ρ_a is a sensitivity-kernel-weighted blend of all the true resistivities the current passed through — not the true value at any one point.

cards.front: What is a pseudosection?
cards.back: A 2-D plot of each reading's apparent resistivity placed horizontally under the array midpoint and vertically at a pseudodepth proportional to electrode separation. It looks like a depth slice but is measurements against a proxy for depth.

cards.front: Why is a pseudosection NOT a model of the earth?
cards.back: Its vertical axis is pseudodepth (a geometric stand-in, not true depth) and its values are apparent, not true, resistivity. To get true resistivity at true depth you must run the inverse problem; the pseudosection is the input to that inversion, not its output.

cards.front: In the data model, how is a pseudosection represented vs. the inverted result?
cards.back: The pseudosection is an Observation with geometryKind 'profile2d'; the inverted result is a separate PropertyModel.

cards.front: What is an electrode 'array', and what tradeoff does it control?
cards.back: An array is the geometric pattern in which the four electrodes are arranged and stepped. It controls the tradeoff between depth reach, lateral resolution, and signal strength.

cards.front: Describe the Wenner array and its strengths/weaknesses.
cards.back: Four equally spaced electrodes A—M—N—B at spacing a, stepped as a set. Strengths: strong signal, robust in noise, good vertical resolution. Weaknesses: poor lateral resolution, slower, shallower reach.

cards.front: Describe the dipole-dipole array and its strengths/weaknesses.
cards.back: A close current pair A B, then a close potential pair M N placed n dipole-lengths away. Strengths: excellent lateral resolution, sees faults/dikes, efficient with multichannel gear. Weaknesses: weaker, noisier signal at large n.

cards.front: Which array does the platform use for ERT/IP, and why?
cards.back: Dipole-dipole, because its lateral sharpness suits mapping a clay cap and faults. (Seen in the 'Type: dipole-dipole' header and array metadata.)

cards.front: How does pseudodepth grow for the dipole-dipole array, and what's the side effect?
cards.back: Pseudodepth grows with the separation n — moving the potential pair farther from the current pair. The same move makes deep readings weaker and noisier.

cards.front: What is ERT's depth of investigation (DOI) rule?
cards.back: DOI ≈ 0.15–0.2 × L_array, where L_array is total electrode-spread length. To see 200 m deep you need roughly a 1–1.3 km line. (Synthetic forward hard-codes DOI = 0.18 × array length.)

cards.front: What happens to ERT sensitivity below the DOI?
cards.back: Sensitivity is killed (a sigmoid cutoff zeroes it out). Beyond the DOI, readings carry no real information and an inversion there is just inventing data.

cards.front: What is ERT good at vs. blind to?
cards.back: Good at: shallow, sharp resolution (clay cap, near-surface faults), mapping fluid/clay zones, high lateral resolution, cheap fast deployment. Blind to: depth (DOI ~15–20% of array length), telling clay from brine, deep reservoir conductor.

cards.front: Why are ERT and MT (magnetotellurics) partners?
cards.back: ERT is sharp but shallow; MT is smooth but reaches kilometres deep. ERT maps the top of the conductive clay cap in detail while MT images the whole cap down to the reservoir.

cards.front: What does IP (induced polarization) measure, and with what gear?
cards.back: IP uses the same four-electrode setup as ERT (often the same survey/.stg file) but measures chargeability — how much the ground temporarily stores charge, like a leaky capacitor.

cards.front: What are the two physical sources of IP chargeability?
cards.back: Disseminated metallic/sulphide grains (electrode polarization at grain surfaces) and clay minerals (membrane polarization in the pore network).

cards.front: How does IP resolve the clay-vs-brine ambiguity that ERT cannot?
cards.back: Both clay and brine look conductive (low ρ) to ERT, but clay is strongly chargeable and brine is not. High conductivity + high chargeability → clay; high conductivity without chargeability → fluid.

cards.front: What are the three IP 'keys' (property, domain, unit)?
cards.back: chargeability_time_ms (time-domain, ms) = decay time of residual voltage; chargeability_mv_v (time-domain, mV/V) = residual voltage as a fraction of primary voltage; phase_mrad (frequency-domain, mrad) = phase lag between AC current and voltage.

cards.front: How do time-domain and frequency-domain IP relate (DSP analogy)?
cards.back: Time-domain IP is like an impulse response: cut the current and watch voltage decay (ms or mV/V). Frequency-domain IP is like a transfer function: drive AC at various frequencies and measure phase lag (mrad). Same property — chargeability — from two viewpoints.

cards.front: How does IP's effective depth reach compare to co-located ERT?
cards.back: IP shares ERT's geometry and DOI (≈0.15–0.2 × array length), but IP data is noisier and the noise worsens with depth, so IP's effective depth reach is usually a bit shallower than the co-located ERT.

cards.front: What native file format do ERT and IP ingest from, and what distinguishes the two?
cards.back: An AGI SuperSting .stg text file. ERT and IP produce the same column layout; only the declared 'value:' quantity differs (e.g. apparent_resistivity_ohm_m vs apparent_chargeability_mv_v).

cards.front: In an .stg record, what geometry is stored per reading?
cards.back: The full quadrupole: A_x,A_y and B_x,B_y (current electrodes), M_x,M_y and N_x,N_y (potential electrodes), the pseudodepth, and the value. The adapter plots the midpoint of the four electrodes at pseudodepth below surface.

cards.front: How does the platform tell ERT and IP .stg files apart?
cards.back: Both adapters sniff() the same columns but disambiguate on the 'value:' label: a resistivity label scores high for the ERT adapter and low for IP (and vice-versa for chargeability/phase). Highest score wins.

cards.front: What normalized primitive does each .stg become, and what produces the true-depth model?
cards.back: Each .stg → one Observation(profile2d) with the apparent value (resistivity or chargeability). Running the inverse problem yields a PropertyModel on a 'section' support (a 2-D curtain along the survey line), or a 'volume' for a 3-D survey.

cards.front: What physical property do electromagnetic (EM) surveys map?
cards.back: The Earth's electrical conductivity σ (S/m) — equivalently resistivity ρ = 1/σ (Ω·m). They map it without ever inserting electrodes into the ground.

cards.front: What single physics fact underlies all EM survey methods?
cards.back: A changing magnetic field induces electric (eddy) currents in a conductor (Faraday's law). The way those currents grow/decay/respond reveals how conductive the rock is and how deep the conductor lies.

cards.front: How does a conductor's quality affect induced eddy currents?
