cards.back: The better the conductor, the bigger and longer-lived the eddy currents. We measure the secondary magnetic field of those currents; its decay/frequency-response is a fingerprint of the ground's conductivity.

cards.front: Galvanic vs. inductive: how do electrical (ERT/IP) and electromagnetic (TEM/MT) methods drive current?
cards.back: Electrical methods are galvanic — wires + electrodes inject current directly, needing good electrode contact. EM methods are inductive — a changing magnetic field induces current with no electrical contact (works over dry/resistive ground, ice, or from a helicopter).

cards.front: What structure and depth range are EM methods (TEM/AEM/MT) best at imaging?
cards.back: Conductive structure, deeper and smoother — depth reach from hundreds of metres (TEM) to tens of kilometres (MT). (Electrical methods instead excel at shallow, sharp, resistive structure.)

cards.front: Why is recovering conductivity from EM data described as a lossy-compression / inverse problem?
cards.back: You inject (or nature injects) a spectrum; the Earth acts as a filter whose coefficients are the conductivity-vs-depth profile, and you record the response. Recovering the filter is an inverse problem — like JPEG decompression, you can never recover more detail than the physics let in.

cards.front: No EM method measures the true conductivity-vs-depth array directly — what does it return instead?
cards.back: A transform of it: TEM/AEM returns apparent conductivity vs depth (a smeared, weighted average over everything shallower), and MT returns apparent resistivity & phase vs period (period being a blurred proxy for depth). Deeper = more blur.

cards.front: What is TEM (TDEM), and what do you record?
cards.back: Time-domain ElectroMagnetics: you drive a strong current through a ground wire loop, switch it off abruptly, and the collapsing field induces eddy currents. You record the decay of the secondary magnetic field over time (microseconds to milliseconds).

cards.front: How does AEM differ from TEM?
cards.back: AEM (Airborne EM) is the same TEM physics flown under a helicopter or fixed wing — a transmitter loop and receiver coil towed over terrain, mapping huge areas fast. Only the platform changes; the physics is identical.

cards.front: What is FDEM, and how does it relate to TEM?
cards.back: Frequency-domain EM: instead of switching off and watching decay, you transmit a continuous sine wave and measure the response per frequency. TEM and FDEM are Fourier transforms of each other — same information, time vs frequency.

cards.front: What is the TEM "smoke ring," and why is it diffusion not propagation?
cards.back: After the transmitter current is cut, the induced eddy currents form a ring that diffuses downward and outward through the ground over time. It's diffusion (like heat or ink in water) — no sharp wavefront — because in a conductor EM energy spreads and decays rather than propagating as a wave.

cards.front: Formula: TEM depth of investigation (DOI) vs elapsed time?
cards.back: DOI ∝ √(t·ρ / μ₀), where t = elapsed time since switch-off (s), ρ = ground resistivity (Ω·m), μ₀ = 4π×10⁻⁷ H/m. Deeper as the decay progresses.

cards.front: TEM decay gates: what's the key intuition linking decay time to depth?
cards.back: Later in the decay = deeper you see. Each decay gate (a time sample of the decay curve) is one depth slice: early gates (~10 µs) sense shallow ground, late gates (~10 ms) sense deep ground. One station records a whole conductivity-depth sounding.

cards.front: Does the smoke ring diffuse faster through resistive or conductive ground?
cards.back: Faster through resistive ground (large ρ) — so over a resistor you reach a given depth sooner (consistent with DOI ∝ √(tρ/μ₀)).

cards.front: What can TEM/AEM see well, and what are its main limitations?
cards.back: Can: map a conductive layer (clay cap, saline aquifer, ore) over wide areas fast, even airborne, with excellent shallow-to-mid resolution. Can't: see very deep (signal decays into noise after ~few hundred m to ~1 km) and it blurs in depth. It's also "conductor-hungry" — a thin conductor can hide what's beneath it (the shielding problem).

cards.front: What is the TEM "shielding" problem?
cards.back: TEM is conductor-hungry: a thin conductive layer dominates the response and can mask whatever lies beneath it, so deeper structure under a strong shallow conductor is hard to see.

cards.front: What does a TEM .xyz sounding file contain per row, and what primitive does it become?
cards.back: Rows of (STATION, X, Y, TIME_S, DEPTH_M, APP_COND_S_per_m) — one block per station. After ingestion each row is one observation at (x, y, depth), collected into a single Observation of geometryKind = "soundings" carrying canonical conductivity (S/m).

cards.front: When stitching 1-D TEM/MT soundings into a 3-D volume, why is DOI-masking applied?
cards.back: Inversion interpolates laterally between stations but masks below each sounding's depth of investigation, so the platform never silently extrapolates into depths the data couldn't actually reach.

cards.front: What is magnetotellurics (MT) and what is its source?
cards.back: MT is a passive EM method using the Earth's natural, naturally-occurring EM fields as its source — no transmitter at all. You plant electrodes and magnetometers and record the naturally varying electric field E and magnetic field H for hours to days.

cards.front: Where do MT's natural source fields come from across the period range?
cards.back: Long periods (~1 s to thousands of s): solar wind buffeting the magnetosphere (geomagnetic storms/sub-storms). Short periods (~0.001–1 s): worldwide lightning ringing the planet in the Schumann resonances. Together they give a free broadband source.

cards.front: What is the MT impedance tensor Z?
cards.back: For each frequency, Z is the 2×2 complex tensor relating the two horizontal E components to the two horizontal H components: [Ex; Ey] = [[Zxx, Zxy],[Zyx, Zyy]]·[Hx; Hy]. It's essentially a transfer-function: measure input H and output E and divide.

cards.front: Formulas: apparent resistivity and phase from MT impedance Z?
cards.back: ρₐ = |Z|² / (ω·μ₀) — apparent resistivity (Ω·m), the resistivity of a uniform earth giving that Z. φ = arg Z — phase (degrees), the lag between E and H. Here ω = 2π/T (rad/s).

cards.front: What does the MT phase φ tell you, and what does 45° mean?
cards.back: φ is essentially the derivative of the apparent-resistivity curve: φ > 45° means resistivity is decreasing with depth (entering a conductor); φ < 45° means increasing (entering a resistor); φ = 45° means a uniform earth.

cards.front: Formula and meaning: MT skin depth δ?
cards.back: δ = 503·√(ρT) metres, where ρ = resistivity (Ω·m) and T = period (s) = 1/frequency. It's the depth at which the field falls to 1/e (~37%) of its surface value — the depth MT effectively "sees" at that period. The 503 bundles √(1/(πμ₀)).

cards.front: Why does MT period T map to depth?
cards.back: Via skin depth δ = 503√(ρT): long periods (slow variations) diffuse deeper before attenuating, so they see deep; short periods see shallow. Period T is a dial for depth. A survey records ~30 periods (~0.001–1000 s), giving one station a sounding from near-surface to deep crust.

cards.front: For a 100 Ω·m host, roughly what depth does a 0.001 s vs a 1000 s MT period sense?
cards.back: δ = 503√(ρT): at T=0.001 s, δ ≈ 159 m (shallow cover/clay cap); at T=1000 s, δ ≈ 159,000 m ≈ 159 km (upper mantle). 0.1 s ≈ 1.6 km (reservoir), 10 s ≈ 16 km (mid-crust).

cards.front: Why is MT THE method for the deep geothermal reservoir conductor?
cards.back: MT is the only common exploration method that reaches kilometres deep (long periods). The deep reservoir is hot saline brine = very low resistivity (a strong conductor), exactly what MT detects at depth — no other technique reaches it.

cards.front: Why is MT deep but low-resolution (the "resolution tax")?
cards.back: Each period averages resistivity over the entire interval [0, δ] (a diffusion average, not a sharp sample), so depth resolution degrades with depth — MT is a strong low-pass filter in depth. It tells you a deep conductor exists, not its exact top, thickness, or sharpness.

cards.front: The ERT-vs-MT depth split: how do they each see the shallow clay cap vs the deep reservoir conductor?
cards.back: Shallow clay cap: ERT sees it sharply (its strength), MT sees it smoothly (shortest periods, blurred). Deep reservoir conductor: ERT can't see it (below its DOI), MT lights it up (its domain). Their disagreement at depth is physically correct, not a bug.

cards.front: Why is ERT/MT disagreement at depth a deliberate fusion test rather than an error?
cards.back: It's physically required: ERT goes silent below its depth of investigation while MT lights up the deep conductor. A correct fusion must honour each method's true depth window rather than blindly averaging, so reproducing this disagreement tests that fusion behaves correctly.

cards.front: What file format carries MT data, and which blocks matter most?
cards.back: EDI (Electrical Data Interchange, an SEG/EMAP standard) — a flat text file of >BLOCK directives. The minimal triad parsed is >FREQ (frequency axis in Hz, high→low), >RHOXY (apparent resistivity Ω·m), and >PHSXY (phase in degrees), plus >HEAD and >=DEFINEMEAS/REFLOC for identity and location.

cards.front: In an EDI file, what is REFLOC and the EMPTY value?
cards.back: REFLOC=x,y (in the >=DEFINEMEAS block) gives the site's plan position in metres, read into the Engineering Frame. EMPTY (in >HEAD, e.g. 1.0E+32) is the "no data" sentinel flagging missing samples.

cards.front: Why is EDI's >FREQ written high-to-low, and how does frequency relate to depth?
cards.back: EDI convention writes frequencies high→low. Since frequency = 1/T, high frequency = short period = shallow; low frequency = long period = deep.

cards.front: What primitive does an MT EDI become after ingestion?
cards.back: One Observation of geometryKind = "tensor" at site (x, y, 0), split into canonical keys resistivity (apparent ρ, Ω·m) and phase_mrad (phase, ingested as degrees and canonicalized to milliradians). The frequency axis rides in metadata; each period is one record.

cards.front: How does MT ingestion embody the "never silently wrong" stance for missing blocks?
cards.back: Missing >RHOXY fails loudly (you can't have an MT sounding without apparent resistivity); missing >PHSXY degrades gracefully with a structured warning; missing REFLOC places the site at the origin and warns.

cards.front: What is the T0 vs T1 forward-model split, and what stays invariant between them?
cards.back: T0 are fast approximations (TEM smoke-ring weighted average, MT skin-depth box average) to exercise the pipeline; T1 is physically exact. Crucially the file format and data model never change — only the fidelity of the physics producing the numbers. "Swap the model, keep the contract."

cards.front: How does the rigorous (T1) MT forward compute true impedance?
cards.back: MTRigorousForward / layered_mt_impedance treats the resistivity column as a stack of layers and computes the true surface plane-wave impedance via the Wait/Cagniard recursion (the analytic kx→0 plane-wave limit of empymod's layered solver), using per-layer wavenumber kᵢ = √(iωμ₀/ρᵢ) and intrinsic impedance Zᵢ = iωμ₀/kᵢ. It emits the same EDI files.

cards.front: In the rigorous MT forward, how does per-layer wavenumber connect back to skin depth?
cards.back: The per-layer skin depth is exactly 1/Re(kᵢ) = 503√(ρᵢ·T) — the same skin-depth equation as the approximation, now computed exactly within the layered Wait/Cagniard recursion.

cards.front: How does the TEM T0 synthetic generator implement the smoke-ring blur?
cards.back: For each of 20 log-spaced gates (10⁻⁵ to 10⁻² s) it computes diffusion depth d = √(2tρ/μ₀), then reports the depth-weighted average conductivity over [0,d] using a triangular weight w = clip(1 − depth/d, 0, 1) that down-weights the deeper, less-resolved part. That weighted average IS the blur; 3–8% Gaussian noise plus a late-time floor are added.

cards.front: What two survey methods does this page cover, and what trait do they share?
cards.back: Geology maps and geochemistry. Both are surface, human-centric data (not instrument cubes/3D volumes): geology maps are vector geometry plus interpretation, and geochemistry is point samples plus lab numbers. Both anchor and constrain the volumes that geophysical methods produce.

cards.front: What is a geological map, fundamentally, and what role does it play in the data stack?
cards.back: It's a geologist's human interpretation turned into geometry (lines and polygons tagged with meaning) — the structural skeleton/framework that every other dataset hangs on. It is the structural framework that constrains the other (geophysical) datasets.

cards.front: Define a geological *contact*.
cards.back: The boundary line/surface where one rock unit meets another (e.g. where alluvium gives way to volcanics) — the geological equivalent of a class boundary.

cards.front: Define a *fault* and why faults matter so much in geothermal systems.
cards.back: A fracture in the rock across which the two sides have moved relative to each other. They matter because faults are often the *plumbing* of a geothermal system — the cracks (permeability) that let hot water rise, acting as fluid conduits.

cards.front: Name the three fault classifications and the stress/motion each implies.
cards.back: Normal (extension — one side drops), reverse (compression — one side rides up), and strike-slip (sideways motion).

cards.front: In the flagship scenario, what is the role of the range-front normal fault?
cards.back: It is both the master structure and the fluid conduit of the geothermal system.

cards.front: Define a rock *unit* (lithology).
cards.back: A body/area of rock of one type and age (e.g. sandstone, granite), drawn on a map as a polygon.

cards.front: Why is a geological map called "2.5D" rather than 2D or 3D?
cards.back: It's genuine 2D plan-view geometry (x, y, no inherent depth) given a single elevation value per point by draping it onto the terrain/surface model: z(x,y) = surfaceModel(x,y). It's not full 3D because there's only one z per point.

cards.front: What does it mean that a fault on a map is only its "surface trace"?
cards.back: The mapped line is just where the fault plane intersects the ground. The fault actually dips down into the earth as a plane, but that 3D extent isn't in the map — it must be modelled later.

cards.front: What three steps does ingestion apply to turn a geologist's vector drawing into typed Features?
cards.back: (1) Reproject it to the Engineering Frame, (2) drape it onto the surfaceModel to assign z, and (3) classify each object into a typed GeologicalFeature (line/surface features for contacts/faults, unit solids only if a 3D geomodel exists).

cards.front: Why can a flat map alone not produce watertight 3D unit solids?
cards.back: A flat map only gives surface features. Turning rock units into solid 3D bodies needs a downstream geomodel (e.g. GemPy) — the map adapter flags this requirement rather than fabricating the solids.

cards.front: What kind of uncertainty tag do map features carry, and how must it be treated?
cards.back: An "interpretive" uncertainty tag — a qualitative confidence tier (low/medium/high), not a number, because a map line is a human opinion, not a measurement. You should never multiply an interpretive line into a precise-looking probability.

cards.front: On a map, what's the difference between a solid contact line and a dashed "inferred" one?
cards.back: A solid line is where the contact is exposed and certain (e.g. a road cut); a dashed "inferred" line is a guess where the contact is buried under soil.

cards.front: What is the core idea behind a geothermometer?
cards.back: Deep hot water sits in chemical equilibrium with surrounding minerals at the reservoir temperature. As it rises quickly, the chemistry "freezes in" (reactions too slow to re-equilibrate), so surface water still carries its last deep equilibrium signature. A geothermometer is an empirical equation that decodes that signature back into a reservoir temperature.

cards.front: Why does the chemistry of rising geothermal water "freeze in"?
cards.back: The water rises to the surface too fast for the temperature-dependent mineral reactions to re-equilibrate on the way up, so the sample preserves the chemical signature of its last deep, hot equilibrium.

cards.front: Write the silica (quartz) geothermometer equation and what it tracks.
cards.back: T(°C) = 1309 / (5.19 − log₁₀(SiO₂)) − 273.15, where SiO₂ is dissolved silica in mg/kg. It tracks quartz solubility; the −273.15 converts the kelvin-based fit to °C.

cards.front: Write the Na–K geothermometer equation and what it uses.
cards.back: T(°C) = 1217 / (log₁₀(Na/K) + 1.483) − 273.15, where Na/K is the molar ratio of sodium to potassium. The ratio re-equilibrates slowly, so it reflects deep temperatures.

cards.front: Why do geochemists run several different geothermometers instead of just one?
cards.back: Different geothermometers (silica, Na–K, Na–K–Ca, gas ratios) re-equilibrate at different rates and depths, so they look for agreement. Disagreement itself is information — it can signal mixing with shallow groundwater or slow cooling.

cards.front: What does a geothermometer-derived temperature feed into, and why is it valuable?
cards.back: It feeds straight into the heat side of favorability (rock physics). It's valuable because it gives a reservoir temperature kilometres below — and it's cheap compared to drilling.

cards.front: Name the three vector GIS container formats for geological maps and their one-line distinctions.
cards.back: Shapefile — legacy format, actually a bundle (.shp/.shx/.dbf/.prj, with CRS in the .prj). GeoJSON — JSON-based single text file, human-readable (what the synthetic generator emits). GeoPackage — a SQLite database (.gpkg), one file/many layers, the modern default.

cards.front: What does each "Feature" in a GeoJSON map consist of?
cards.back: One geometry (LineString, Point, or Polygon) plus a properties attribute table (e.g. kind, faultKind, uncertainty, or unitA/unitB for a contact).

cards.front: In synthetic/local mode, what coordinate system are GeoJSON coordinates already in?
cards.back: Engineering metres (East, North). In a real file they're in the source CRS and get reprojected on ingest; in synthetic/local mode they're already Engineering metres.

cards.front: What is a LIMS export and how is geochemistry data structured in it?
cards.back: LIMS = Laboratory Information Management System. Its export is a tabular CSV: one row per sample, with columns for location (x, y, elev) and each measured species (SiO2, Na, K, Ca, Cl, pH, etc.).

cards.front: In a geochem CSV, what is `T_emer_C` and how does it differ from the reservoir temperature?
cards.back: T_emer_C is the temperature measured *at the surface* (emergence) with a thermometer — NOT the reservoir temperature. The reservoir temperature is *computed* from SiO2 and Na/K via the geothermometer equations, and that computed value is the prize.

cards.front: What are the units of the dissolved species concentrations in the geochem table, and which feed the geothermometers?
cards.back: Concentrations are in mg/kg. SiO2, Na, K, Ca, and Cl are the dissolved species that feed the geothermometers (SiO2 → silica geothermometer; Na/K → Na–K geothermometer).

cards.front: What normalized primitive does a geochem sample row become, and how is it shaped?
cards.back: An ObservationSet with geometryKind "points" — one point per sample at an (x, y, z) location, with the measured assays as columns. Samples are often placed at the surface, or at a well's MD if from a well.

cards.front: What normalized primitives do fault traces, contacts, and rock units become after ingestion?
cards.back: Fault trace → GeologicalFeature featureKind "fault" (with FaultDetail: faultType, dip, throw). Contact → GeologicalFeature featureKind "surface" (a draped interpreted surface). Rock unit polygon → "surface", or "unitSolid" only if a geomodel exists.

cards.front: Why are faults kept as Features rather than baked directly into the voxel grid?
cards.back: A fault is a discrete shape someone interpreted, with editable geometry and an audit trail — the Feature primitive's job. Baking it into a grid would destroy the interpretation and freeze a resampling choice; Features stay sharp and re-editable, and the fused grid samples *from* them when needed.

cards.front: What downstream consumers use the map Features versus the geothermometer temperatures?
cards.back: The typed Features (faults, surfaces, unit solids) supply the structural skeleton that constrains fusion; the geothermometer temperatures feed the heat term of favorability.

cards.front: Contrast the two methods on this page in terms of what they output: sparse vs. dense, rich in what?
cards.back: Geology maps are vector geometry (lines/polygons) — the structural framework. Geochemistry is sparse (a handful of point locations) but chemically rich (a full table of measured concentrations per point). Neither produces a 3D volume on its own.

cards.front: Why must a geothermal explorer measure the Earth indirectly?
cards.back: You cannot see through kilometres of rock, so the Earth is measured indirectly from the surface, air, satellite, or borehole using physics. Each survey method is a sensor that responds to a different physical property of the rock.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: In the 'lossy, band-limited sensor' CS analogy, what four sensor characteristics does each survey method have?
cards.back: Each method has its own sampling rate (station spacing), bandwidth (smallest feature size it resolves), dynamic range (what it's sensitive to at all), and noise floor. Reconstructing the true earth from these degraded measurements is an inverse problem with many possible answers.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What is the forward problem in geophysics?
cards.back: Given the earth, predict the measurement — e.g. 'if the rock has density ρ, what gravity reading appears?' It is a well-defined function (a simulator): one earth in, one noisy dataset out. Written F(m) = d.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What is the inverse problem in geophysics?
cards.back: Given the measurement, recover the earth — e.g. 'what density distribution produced these gravity values?' It runs the physics backwards (F⁻¹(d) = m) and is hard, slow, and ambiguous. It is the central subject of inversion.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: In the equation m → F(m) → d, what do m, d, and F represent?
cards.back: m is the earth model (e.g. a 3-D density volume), d is the data (e.g. gravity readings), and F is the physics that turns the model into the data. F (forward) is unique and computable; F⁻¹ (inverse) generally does not exist as a clean function.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: Why does the inverse operator F⁻¹ cause trouble compared to the forward operator F?
cards.back: The forward operator F is unique and computable, but F⁻¹ generally does not exist as a clean function — many different earths can map to the same data — which leads directly to non-uniqueness.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: Compiler analogy: how do forward and inverse problems map to running programs?
cards.back: The forward problem is like running a program to get output. The inverse problem is like staring at the output and reconstructing the source code — many programs print 42, and many different earths produce the same gravity map.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What does non-uniqueness mean?
cards.back: Different earths can produce identical data, so the inverse problem has many solutions and the data alone cannot choose between them. E.g. a small shallow dense body and a large deep dense body can pull on a gravimeter identically.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: Which survey methods are the textbook worst offenders for non-uniqueness?
cards.back: Potential-field methods (gravity and magnetics) are the textbook offenders, but every method is non-unique to some degree.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What are the three ways the platform deals with non-uniqueness?
cards.back: 1) Carry uncertainty everywhere so a number is never over-trusted; 2) Fuse independent methods so one's ambiguity is constrained by another's strength; 3) Regularize the inversion by injecting prior assumptions (smoothness, known geology) to pick a plausible answer.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: Why should you not trust a single crisp inverted resistivity cube?
cards.back: An inverted volume looks definitive but is just one of infinitely many models consistent with the data, chosen by the inversion's assumptions. Treat every inverted property model as a hypothesis, not a photograph.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What is depth of investigation (DOI)?
cards.back: DOI is how deep a method can actually 'see' with usable signal. Below the DOI the measurement contains essentially no information about the rock — sensitivity has decayed to noise, and anything an inversion shows there comes from its regularization, not the data.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: In signal terms, where exactly is the depth of investigation (DOI)?
cards.back: DOI is the depth where the signal-to-noise ratio crosses 1. Below it the data is effectively silent.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: How does the platform handle data below a sounding's DOI during ingestion?
cards.back: When 1-D soundings are stitched into a 3-D volume, everything below each sounding's DOI is masked and flagged low-confidence rather than silently extrapolated.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What is the rough DOI of ERT (electrical resistivity), and why?
cards.back: Roughly 15–20% of the electrode array length, because current spreads only so far from finite electrodes.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: Why do gravity and magnetics have a DOI of 'everything, blurrily'?
cards.back: The measured field sums all mass/magnetization at once with no inherent depth resolution, so they sense everything but cannot localize it in depth.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What controls the depth of investigation in magnetotellurics (MT)?
cards.back: Frequency, via the skin effect — giving a DOI ranging from tens of metres to tens of kilometres depending on the frequencies used.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What is a resolution kernel (sensitivity kernel / footprint)?
cards.back: A real sensor reports a weighted average of the true property over a region, not a single point; the resolution kernel is the shape of that weighting. A small sharp kernel = high resolution; a broad kernel = a blurry, smoothed image.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: How is a resolution kernel mathematically like a point spread function?
cards.back: The measurement is the true earth convolved with the kernel: d(x) = ∫ K(x,x') m(x') dx'. Inversion is partly an attempt to deconvolve the kernel, which (like image deconvolution) amplifies noise and is unstable — another face of non-uniqueness.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What shape is the ERT forward model's resolution kernel, and how does it behave with depth?
cards.back: Each apparent-resistivity reading is a Gaussian-sensitivity-weighted average of the true resistivity column beneath the electrode array — a depth-decaying 'banana'-shaped kernel that fades out below the DOI.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: Distinguish lateral resolution from vertical resolution.
cards.back: Lateral resolution is the smallest horizontal feature you can distinguish, set mostly by station/line spacing (a sampling-rate question). Vertical resolution is the smallest vertical feature, set by physics and usually degrading with depth as the kernel broadens.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What are the three normalized data primitives every survey method collapses into?
cards.back: Observation (immutable raw measurement tied to where/when), PropertyModel (a continuous field of one physical property), and GeologicalFeature (a discrete interpreted shape like a fault or well path).
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What is an Observation primitive and what does it carry?
cards.back: The immutable raw measurement tied to where/when it was taken. It carries a geometryKind ∈ {points, soundings, profile2d, traces, raster2d, wellcurve, tensor}.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What is a PropertyModel primitive and what does it carry?
cards.back: A continuous field of one physical property. It carries a support.kind ∈ {volume, grid2d, section, mesh}.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: When does an ingested method become a PropertyModel directly versus an Observation?
cards.back: A method arriving already inverted (e.g. a finished resistivity cube) becomes a PropertyModel directly. A method arriving raw (e.g. gravity station readings) becomes an Observation, and only later — via an explicit provenance-tracked step — produces a PropertyModel. 'Raw stays raw.'
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What three things must coincide for a geothermal target, and why each?
cards.back: Heat (hot rock/fluid), fluid (water to carry the heat), and permeability (cracks for the fluid to flow). Geothermal success means finding all three in the same place.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What physical property does gravity sense, and what is its geothermal relevance?
cards.back: Gravity senses bulk density, revealing basin shape, faults, intrusions, and voids. Dense intrusions can indicate a possible heat source — it gives structure.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What does magnetics sense, and what is the key geothermal fingerprint it provides?
cards.back: Magnetics senses magnetic susceptibility. A magnetic low over a demagnetized hydrothermal alteration zone is a heat/fluid fingerprint.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What does ERT (DC resistivity) sense, and what geothermal feature is it best for?
cards.back: ERT senses electrical resistivity, giving a shallow, sharp map of the conductive clay cap that seals a reservoir (a fluid + heat indicator), plus faults.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What does IP (induced polarization) sense, and what does it distinguish?
cards.back: IP senses chargeability, revealing disseminated clay and sulphide minerals. It distinguishes conductive clay from conductive brine — useful for alteration mapping.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: Why is magnetotellurics (MT) called the flagship/workhorse geothermal method?
cards.back: MT senses deep electrical resistivity, imaging a conductor down to many km, revealing the deep conductive clay cap above a reservoir — though its image is smooth.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What does microseismic (passive seismic) sense, and what does it provide direct evidence of?
cards.back: It senses tiny earthquakes from fracture activity, showing where rock is cracking/moving now. It is direct (4-D) evidence of permeability and active fractures.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What does InSAR measure, and what is its geothermal use?
cards.back: InSAR measures surface deformation at mm scale (ground swelling/sinking over time), revealing pressure/volume change in a reservoir — used for 4-D monitoring.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What is the most direct measurement of geothermal heat, and what does it give?
cards.back: Temperature / heat-flow logging, which measures temperature vs depth and gives the geothermal gradient directly.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What role do well logs play among survey methods?
cards.back: Well logs provide ground-truth rock properties (resistivity, density, porosity) at the borehole, serving as calibration 'ground truth' that ties surface surveys to reality — but only along a thin line.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What is the key pattern in the method → property → geothermal-relevance map?
cards.back: No single method covers all three of heat + fluid + permeability. Each method's blind spot is another's strength (gravity is deep but blurry, ERT shallow but sharp, MT deep but smooth, seismic structural not thermal, wells true but thin), so fusion exploits this complementarity.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What two transformations happen to native survey files on ingestion?
cards.back: Coordinates are reprojected into the project's Engineering Frame (local X-East/Y-North/Z-Up metre grid), and units are canonicalized (mGal, nT, ohm·m, kelvin…) by a units registry, with the native unit recorded in provenance so nothing is silently mis-scaled.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What 8-part shared template does every method page follow?
cards.back: 1) The physics, 2) what property it senses, 3) what it can and can't see, 4) depth of investigation & resolution, 5) native file format annotated, 6) the normalized primitive it becomes, 7) where it's strong for geothermal, 8) key takeaways and where it lives in the code.
cards.page_title: The Survey Methods — How to read these pages

cards.front: What does InSAR stand for, and what does it measure?
cards.back: Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar. It measures the change in distance between a satellite and the ground between two radar passes — i.e. surface ground deformation (uplift/subsidence) to millimetre precision — by comparing the phase of the reflected radar wave.

cards.front: How does InSAR differ from other survey methods in imaging the subsurface?
cards.back: Unlike methods that image the rock directly, InSAR watches only the surface and lets you infer what's happening kilometres below. Surface movement is a fingerprint of pressure changes in the reservoir.

cards.front: Why is InSAR called the '4D' / canonical time-axis dataset?
cards.back: A satellite passes over the same spot repeatedly, so InSAR yields a time-series of deformation images (a movie of the ground), not a single snapshot — 2D rasters plus a time axis = 4D.

cards.front: What two things come back in a radar echo, and what does each tell you?
cards.back: Amplitude — how much energy bounced back (bright = buildings/rock, dark = water/smooth); this is the ordinary radar image. Phase — where in its cycle the returning wave is, giving round-trip distance modulo one wavelength. Phase is the ingredient that enables deformation measurement.

cards.front: What is Sentinel-1's radar wavelength λ?
cards.back: Approximately 56 mm. A known constant of the satellite used to convert phase to distance.

cards.front: What is an interferogram?
cards.back: The map produced by subtracting the phase of two radar passes — it shows how much each ground point moved along the satellite's viewing direction (line of sight) between the passes.

cards.front: Why does differencing two phase measurements give millimetre precision when a single pass only locates a point to a few metres?
cards.back: The difference cancels almost everything common to both passes (bulk distance, nominal orbit) and leaves only what changed — the tiny deformation. It's the same trick as a version-control diff: absolute files are huge and mostly identical; the delta is small and is all you care about.

cards.front: Formula: how does line-of-sight displacement relate to measured phase difference?
cards.back: d_LOS = (λ / 4π) · Δφ, where λ = radar wavelength (m), Δφ = phase difference (radians), d_LOS = displacement along line of sight (m).

cards.front: Why is the factor 4π (not 2π) in d_LOS = (λ/4π)·Δφ?
cards.back: Because the radar wave makes a round trip — out to the ground and back — so a given ground movement changes the path length by twice that movement.

cards.front: What is phase wrapping, and why is it a problem?
cards.back: Phase is known only modulo 2π (it resets every wavelength, like an angle at 360°), so the raw interferogram is 'wrapped' — smooth deformation appears as repeating colour fringes. Recovering true continuous deformation needs phase unwrapping (counting whole cycles crossed), a hard, error-prone inverse step.

cards.front: Does the platform ingest raw phase or an unwrapped product?
cards.back: It ingests the already-unwrapped, processed product — a deformation raster in millimetres — not raw phase. But that number is the output of a non-trivial, sometimes-wrong reconstruction.

cards.front: What is the line of sight (LOS) in InSAR?
cards.back: The unit vector pointing from a ground point up to the satellite. The satellite looks down at a slant (typically 30–45° off vertical), so the LOS is a mix of vertical and horizontal directions. InSAR measures movement along LOS, NOT vertical movement.

cards.front: Formula: how does InSAR's reported d_LOS relate to true 3D ground displacement?
cards.back: d_LOS = u⃗·l̂ = u_E·l_E + u_N·l_N + u_U·l_U — a dot product (projection) of the 3D displacement u⃗=(u_E,u_N,u_U) onto the LOS unit vector l̂=(l_E,l_N,l_U). In the Engineering Frame X=East, Y=North, Z=Up.

cards.front: Why is the LOS measurement called 'lossy'?
cards.back: You get one scalar per pixel, not the full 3D motion. A purely horizontal slide and a purely vertical lift can produce the same LOS reading, so InSAR alone can't distinguish them.

cards.front: How do you recover full 3D motion from InSAR?
cards.back: Use multiple viewing geometries — e.g. an ascending track (satellite heading north) and a descending track (heading south), whose LOS vectors differ — then solve the small linear system.

cards.front: With LOS vertical component l_U = 0.79, how does a true 10 mm vertical uplift appear in the LOS image, and why does it matter?
cards.back: It appears as only 10 × 0.79 = 7.9 mm. If you read 7.9 mm as the real uplift you underestimate the reservoir pressure change by ~20%. This is why the LOS vector must be stored with the data and travel into any inversion.

cards.front: What is Sentinel-1's revisit cadence, and why can't you assume a fixed Δt between InSAR frames?
cards.back: Every 12 days. But passes are irregular in time (orbits skipped, weather kills some interferograms), so explicit calendar dates (ISO-8601 UTC) are stored per pass; never assume a fixed cadence.

cards.front: How is an InSAR dense product indexed as a data structure?
cards.back: A 3D array indexed [t, y, x]: t = epoch index (which pass), y/x = raster row/column on a regular ground grid. It's a 2D raster with a leading time axis — exactly a video. The platform always puts time as the first axis when present.

cards.front: In a geothermal field, what surface effect does fluid INJECTION produce, and why?
cards.back: Uplift (ground bulges up). Raising pore pressure inflates the reservoir, lifting the overburden.

cards.front: In a geothermal field, what surface effect does fluid PRODUCTION produce, and why?
cards.back: Subsidence (ground sinks). Dropping pore pressure compacts the reservoir, so the surface settles.

cards.front: What is a Mogi source?
cards.back: A classic model of the surface deformation from a point pressure change in an elastic half-space. It predicts a radially symmetric uplift bowl (a smooth bell curve) over a small pressurised source at depth.

cards.front: Formula: Mogi source vertical displacement.
cards.back: u_U(r) = [(1−ν)·ΔV / π] · D / (r² + D²)^(3/2), where r = horizontal distance from above the source (m), D = source depth (m), ΔV = source volume change (m³), ν = Poisson's ratio (~0.25).

cards.front: How does Mogi-source bowl shape depend on source depth?
cards.back: Deep source → wide, gentle bowl. Shallow source → tight, peaked bowl. Inferring depth and volume from the surface bowl is itself an inverse problem.

cards.front: What is the Atmospheric Phase Screen (APS), and why is it the hardest InSAR noise to remove?
cards.back: Radar waves slow passing through wet air; if tropospheric water content differs between passes it adds spurious phase. It's spatially correlated (smooth blobs), so it mimics a real deformation signal.

cards.front: What is decorrelation in InSAR?
cards.back: Phase comparison only works if the ground scatters radar the same way on both passes. Vegetation growth, snow, ploughed fields, or long time gaps decorrelate the signal, making phase random noise. Cities and bare rock stay coherent; forests and farmland fall apart.

cards.front: What does orbital / DEM error add to an InSAR scene?
cards.back: Imperfect knowledge of the satellite's orbit or of the ground elevation model adds smooth ramps and tilts across the scene.

cards.front: What are Persistent Scatterers (PS), and what trade-off do they make?
cards.back: Instead of every pixel, keep only points that stay radar-bright and phase-stable across the whole time-series (rock outcrops, buildings, corner reflectors). PS InSAR trades dense coverage for a sparse set of points, each with a clean millimetre-level deformation time-series — delivered as a CSV table, not a raster.

cards.front: In what TWO shapes does InSAR data arrive?
cards.back: (1) Dense rasters — a stack of GeoTIFFs, one per epoch; and (2) sparse Persistent-Scatterer point time-series — a CSV table of points.

cards.front: What is each GeoTIFF in an InSAR time-series stack?
cards.back: One file per satellite pass (los_00.tif … los_NN.tif), a single-band float32 raster of LOS deformation in millimetres, named in epoch order, with an embedded geotransform mapping pixel (col,row) to ground (x,y).

cards.front: Why is GeoTIFF transform.e (metres per row) negative, and what does the adapter do about it?
cards.back: Raster row 0 is the northernmost row (image top-first convention), so y decreases as row increases. The Engineering Frame is Y-north-ascending, so the InSAR adapter flipud's each band — making the first row the southernmost — to give a clean ascending [t, y, x] cube. Same mismatch as screen (top-left origin) vs math (bottom-left origin) coordinates.

cards.front: In a PS CSV, what does the 'los_mean_mm_yr' column represent?
cards.back: The average LOS velocity in mm/year — the headline 'is it moving?' number for each persistent scatterer point.

cards.front: In a PS CSV, what does the 'coherence' column mean?
cards.back: A 0..1 phase-stability score, where 1 = a rock-solid scatterer. It indicates how trustworthy that point's deformation time-series is.

cards.front: Into which two normalized primitives does InSAR land after ingestion?
cards.back: Dense GeoTIFF series → PropertyModel(property='deformation') with support grid2d and a leading t axis ([t,y,x]). PS CSV → ObservationSet of point Observations (geometryKind='points') carrying a TimeAxis of epochs.

cards.front: What canonical unit and colormap does the 'deformation' property type use, and why that colormap?
cards.back: Unit = mm; colormap = RdBu (red/blue diverging) so uplift and subsidence read at a glance around zero.

cards.front: Why must the LOS vector travel with the InSAR dataset into the model?
cards.back: So downstream consumers (fusion, inflation-source inversion) never mistake the LOS scalar for true 3D/vertical displacement — the data carries the reminder that 'this number is a projection, not vertical motion.'

cards.front: What is a potential field?
cards.back: A field that can be written as the gradient of a scalar potential function. Gravity and magnetism are the two classic examples; their potentials obey Laplace's equation (∇²φ = 0) in source-free space above ground.

cards.front: What three properties does being a potential field force on gravity and magnetics?
cards.back: They are (1) smooth — sharp underground boundaries appear as gentle surface gradients; (2) deep-reaching but depth-blind — the field sums all sources at once with no inherent depth resolution; and (3) badly non-unique — a small shallow body and a big deep body can produce identical surface anomalies.

cards.front: Why does burying a source blur its surface signal?
cards.back: Moving the sensor up (or source down) acts like a low-pass blur kernel whose width grows with distance — mathematically identical to upward continuation. By the surface, a deep small body is lossily compressed into a faint broad smudge that can't be uniquely inverted.

cards.front: How do gravity and magnetics differ from each other physically?
cards.back: Only in the source and the controlling property: gravity's source is mass, controlled by density (ρ); magnetics' source is magnetization, controlled by magnetic susceptibility (χ). The Laplace smoothness/blur is the same for both.

cards.front: What physical quantity does gravity surveying measure?
cards.back: Tiny variations in Earth's gravitational pull caused by lateral changes in rock density. Denser rock pulls harder; less dense rock (basins, fractured/altered zones) pulls less.

cards.front: What is the point-mass gravity kernel used in the forward model?
cards.back: g_z = G · Σ (Δρ·V·Δz)/r³, summed over voxels. G = 6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³ kg⁻¹ s⁻², Δρ is density anomaly, V is voxel volume (so Δm = Δρ·V), Δz is vertical voxel-to-sensor distance, r is 3-D distance.

cards.front: In the gravity kernel, why the Δz/r³ factor specifically?
cards.back: It extracts the vertical component of the gravitational pull, because a gravimeter only senses the "down" direction. The gentle 1/r³ falloff is exactly why gravity reaches deep but blurs.

cards.front: What is the density anomaly (Δρ) in gravity, and why does only it matter?
cards.back: Δρ is the rock's density minus the background (per-depth median) density at its depth, in kg/m³. Only lateral contrasts produce an anomaly — a uniform layered earth produces none, because aligning the layers cancels.

cards.front: Define the milligal (mGal) and its SI value.
cards.back: 1 gal = 1 cm/s² = 0.01 m/s². The milligal: 1 mGal = 10⁻³ gal = 10⁻⁵ m/s².

cards.front: How large is a meaningful gravity exploration anomaly relative to Earth's gravity?
cards.back: Earth's gravity ≈ 9.8 m/s² ≈ 980,000 mGal; an exploration anomaly is a few mGal — about one part in a million. Modern gravimeters resolve a few hundredths of a mGal.

cards.front: What is the Bouguer anomaly?
cards.back: The raw gravity reading after stripping out predictable effects — latitude, sensor elevation, and the pull of topographic mass between sensor and datum (the "Bouguer slab"). What remains is the signal of subsurface density contrasts, the thing you actually want.

cards.front: For geothermal work, what is gravity good at and blind to?
cards.back: Good at: basin shape (low-density sediment = gravity low), faults (density steps), and dense intrusions (possible heat source = gravity high). Blind to: depth of features, fine detail, heat directly (density barely changes with temperature), and fluid/permeability unless bulk density changes.

cards.front: What sets gravity's lateral resolution?
cards.back: Station spacing (a sampling-rate problem) and the inherent smoothing of the potential field. Closer stations give finer resolvable features, up to the physics limit; the forward model applies an explicit Gaussian low-pass to the station grid.

cards.front: What two files make up the native gravity ingest pair?
cards.back: A CSV of scattered station readings (gravity_stations.csv, with the bouguer_mgal measurement column) and a Bouguer-anomaly grid as a GeoTIFF (gravity_bouguer.tif) — already interpolated onto a regular 2-D raster.

cards.front: In the gravity GeoTIFF, what does the affine transform do and why is row 0 the northmost?
cards.back: The 2×3 affine matrix maps a pixel (col, row) to ground (x, y) in Engineering metres — the raster equivalent of origin + spacing. Row 0 is northmost because dy is negative (e.g. -250), the rasterio convention, which is why the writer flips the array.

cards.front: What two normalized primitives does the gravity pair become on ingestion?
cards.back: The CSV → an Observation (geometryKind "points", gravity_anomaly in mGal, the immutable raw record — not auto-gridded). The GeoTIFF → a PropertyModel (support.kind "grid2d", gravity_anomaly as a 2-D field). A 3-D density volume only comes later via inversion.

cards.front: Why does turning gravity into a 3-D density volume require inversion with regularization?
cards.back: Going from surface anomaly to a subsurface density volume is the inverse problem, and gravity is badly non-unique, so the inversion needs regularization and ideally constraints from other methods to pick one plausible model.

cards.front: What does magnetic surveying measure, and which mineral dominates?
cards.back: Tiny variations in Earth's magnetic field caused by magnetic minerals in rock — overwhelmingly magnetite. Earth's field induces magnetization proportional to the rock's susceptibility.

cards.front: What is the induced magnetization relationship for magnetics?
cards.back: M = χH = χB₀/μ₀, where χ is magnetic susceptibility (dimensionless), H is the magnetizing field, B₀ is Earth's ambient field (~50,000 nT), and μ₀ is the permeability of free space. Each magnetized voxel then acts as a tiny dipole.

cards.front: What is magnetic susceptibility (χ)?
cards.back: A dimensionless number describing how easily a rock becomes magnetized — the property magnetics actually senses (the magnetic analogue of density for gravity).

cards.front: What is the dipole anomaly kernel used in the magnetics forward model?
cards.back: ΔB_z = (1/4π) · Σ χ·B₀·V·(3Δz² − r²)/r⁵ × 10⁹ nT. B₀ = 50,000 nT; the (3Δz² − r²) term is the dipole kernel shape; ×10⁹ converts tesla to nT.

cards.front: Define the nanotesla (nT) and Earth's field strength.
cards.back: 1 nT = 10⁻⁹ T. Earth's field is roughly 50,000 nT; exploration anomalies range from a few nT to a few thousand nT. Modern magnetometers resolve well under 1 nT.

cards.front: What is reduction to the pole (RTP) and why is it needed?
cards.back: A dipole anomaly is lopsided (skewed by the field's inclination at your latitude), placing the anomaly peak off to one side of its source. RTP mathematically re-poses the data as if measured at the magnetic pole (vertical field), so anomalies sit directly over their sources — much easier to interpret.

cards.front: Why does magnetics see a magnetic LOW over a geothermal upflow zone?
cards.back: Hot fluids cause hydrothermal alteration that destroys magnetite, converting it to non-magnetic minerals and dropping susceptibility χ toward zero. So an active upflow reads as a magnetic low — a demagnetized "hole" — not a high.

cards.front: Why is the magnetic low so diagnostic for geothermal exploration?
cards.back: Most methods see heat or fluid only indirectly. The magnetic low is a chemical fingerprint of past or present hot-fluid flow — magnetics can't see heat, but it can see the alteration scar that heat leaves behind.

cards.front: For geothermal, what is magnetics good at and blind to?
cards.back: Good at: mapping magnetic basement and intrusions, hydrothermal alteration (as a magnetic low), faults, and fast cheap airborne reconnaissance over huge areas. Blind to: depth, heat and fluid directly, non-magnetic structure (no χ contrast = invisible), and sharp detail (worse with flight altitude).

cards.front: What dominates magnetics resolution for airborne surveys?
cards.back: Flight altitude and line spacing — higher and farther apart = blurrier. The forward applies an upward-continuation Gaussian low-pass whose width is proportional to flight altitude. Flying lower and tighter buys sharper maps.

cards.front: Why is airborne magnetic data anisotropic in resolution?
cards.back: Samples are dense along each flight line (small step, e.g. 31.25 m) but coarse across lines (a full line-spacing apart, e.g. 250 m). So along-line is the high-resolution direction; across-line is a real resolution limit.

cards.front: How does the ingestion adapter tell the gravity grid apart from the mag grid?
cards.back: Purely by filename — a bare Float32 grid carries no clue otherwise. Names containing mag/rtp/tmi/aeromag claim the GeoTIFF for magnetics; otherwise it's treated as the gravity (Bouguer) grid.

cards.front: What primitives does the magnetics pair become on ingestion?
cards.back: The .xyz flight-line file → an Observation (points, magnetic_field in nT, with line id in meta). The RTP GeoTIFF → a PropertyModel (grid2d, magnetic_field). Inversion later → a PropertyModel (volume, susceptibility).

cards.front: What role do gravity and magnetics play in a geothermal program?
cards.back: They are the cheap, fast, regional reconnaissance layer — flown early to draw the structural skeleton (basins, faults, intrusions, magnetic basement, and the alteration low) and decide where to spend money on sharper, deeper methods (MT, ERT, seismic). Blurry and non-unique alone, they become powerful when fused with EM/electrical (fluid) and seismic (structure).

cards.front: Why does a flat, layered earth produce a flat gravity map?
cards.back: The forward computes Δρ as density minus the per-depth median density, so aligned layers cancel. Only lateral mass contrasts — a dense intrusion, light fault gouge, or low-density basin fill — survive into the Bouguer anomaly.

cards.front: What physical property does seismic surveying measure, and how does it differ from electromagnetic methods?
cards.back: Seismic measures **seismic velocity** — how fast sound travels through rock. Whereas EM is about how rock conducts electricity, seismic is about how rock carries sound.

cards.front: Define $V_p$ (P-wave velocity), including typical rock values.
cards.back: $V_p$ is the **P-wave (compressional) velocity** in m/s — the speed of a push-pull wave (like sound in air) and the *first* arrival. Typical rock: 2,000–6,000 m/s.

cards.front: Define $V_s$ (S-wave velocity) and what makes it diagnostic of fluids.
cards.back: $V_s$ is the **S-wave (shear) velocity** in m/s — a slower, sideways-shaking wave that **cannot travel through fluids**. The $V_p/V_s$ ratio is therefore a fluid clue.

cards.front: What is acoustic impedance $Z$, and what is the formula?
cards.back: **Acoustic impedance $Z = \rho \cdot V_p$** (density times P-velocity). It is the single most important quantity for reflection seismic.

cards.front: In reflection seismic, what actually produces an echo — $Z$ itself or something else?
cards.back: Echoes come from **changes in $Z$**, not from $Z$ itself. A signal only reflects at an *impedance mismatch* — exactly like an electrical transmission line or optical interface.

cards.front: Reflection seismic is, almost literally, what mathematical operation (programmer's mental model)?
cards.back: A **convolution**: the reflectivity series convolved with a band-limited wavelet. Reflectivity is the normalized first difference of the impedance array; the wavelet is the survey's 'point spread function.'

cards.front: How is reflectivity computed from an impedance array, and where is it nonzero?
cards.back: Reflectivity is the **normalized first difference** of impedance: `(imp[1:] - imp[:-1]) / (imp[1:] + imp[:-1])`. It spikes only where impedance *changes* (a layer contact or fault) and is ~zero between contacts.

cards.front: Why is a recorded seismic trace blurred rather than a series of sharp spikes?
cards.back: The source emits a **band-limited pulse (wavelet)**, not an infinitely sharp spike. Convolving reflectivity with that wavelet blurs the result exactly like a low-pass filter — which sets the vertical resolution limit.

cards.front: What is the data shape of a microseismic dataset?
cards.back: A **4-D point cloud**: $(x, y, z, t, \text{magnitude})$ — one point per micro-earthquake, located in space and stamped in time. It's a stream of events, not a field.

cards.front: Define reflection seismology.
cards.back: Bang the surface (vibrator truck, weight drop, or charge) and record at many geophones the **echoes** bouncing off subsurface impedance contrasts. From echo *times* and *amplitudes* you reconstruct the layered/faulted structure.

cards.front: What is a CMP (Common Mid-Point) in seismic reflection?
cards.back: By combining many source-receiver pairs that share a midpoint, you build one high-quality trace per surface location. Each CMP trace is one 'pixel column' of the final image.

cards.front: What is the vertical axis of a seismic section — depth or something else?
cards.back: **TWT (Two-Way Time)** — the round-trip travel time of the echo, in seconds or ms. It is *not* depth. Down means later in time; converting to depth requires a velocity model.

cards.front: Give the reflection coefficient formula at an impedance interface (normal incidence).
cards.back: $r = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$, where $Z = \rho V_p$. It is dimensionless, bounded in $[-1, 1]$, and is the spike height in the reflectivity series.

cards.front: What wavelet does the project use to build a synthetic seismic trace, and what is its shape?
cards.back: A zero-phase **Ricker** wavelet — the classic symmetric 'Mexican hat' pulse. The synthetic trace is $\text{trace}(t) = r(t) \ast w(t) + \text{noise}$.

cards.front: How do you map a layer's depth onto two-way time?
cards.back: Integrate slowness down the column: each layer of thickness $\Delta z$ adds $2\,\Delta z / V_p$ of round-trip (two-way) time.

cards.front: State the seismic vertical resolution rule ($\lambda/4$) with its formula.
cards.back: $\Delta z_{\min} \approx \dfrac{\lambda}{4} = \dfrac{V_p}{4f}$. Two reflectors closer than ~a quarter-wavelength merge into one. ($\lambda$ = dominant wavelength, $f$ = peak frequency.)

cards.front: Worked example: with $V_p = 3000$ m/s and $f = 30$ Hz, what is the vertical resolution?
cards.back: $\lambda = V_p/f = 100$ m, so $\Delta z_{\min} \approx \lambda/4 = 25$ m. Beds thinner than ~25 m blur together at that depth.

cards.front: Why is deep seismic imaging inherently lower-resolution?
cards.back: Higher frequency gives finer resolution ($\Delta z_{\min} = V_p/4f$), but high frequencies **attenuate faster**, so only lower frequencies survive to depth — making deep images coarser. It's analogous to a sampling/Nyquist bound.

cards.front: Why is reflection seismic nearly blind to fluid and temperature? (the key conceptual point)
cards.back: Reflectivity comes from impedance *contrasts*, which are dominated by lithology (layer contacts, faults). Temperature and pore-fluid change impedance only weakly by comparison, so seismic shows you **where the container is, not what's in it or how hot it is** — which is why fusion with EM/borehole methods is needed.

cards.front: What does the T1 'rigorous' reflection forward add over the T0 convolutional model?
cards.back: T1 uses the **full impedance series** (every depth sample, not hand-picked contacts), **amplitude-conserving linear placement** of each reflection at its true two-way time, and adds a first-order surface **multiple** (delayed, polarity-flipped echo). Both T0 and T1 emit the same SEG-Y + horizons GeoJSON.

cards.front: What is SEG-Y and what are its three structural sections?
cards.back: **SEG-Y** is the universal (binary) seismic exchange format from the SEG. Layout: (1) **Textual header** (3200 bytes, 40×80-char lines of human notes), (2) **Binary header** (400 bytes of survey-wide fields), (3) **per-trace** records (240-byte trace header + ns 4-byte float samples).

cards.front: In a SEG-Y file, what do `dt`, `ns`, and `data_format = 5` mean?
cards.back: `dt` = sample interval in microseconds (sets the TWT axis spacing); `ns` = samples per trace (trace length in time); `data_format = 5` = IEEE 4-byte float32 (how to decode sample bytes).

cards.front: What do the SEG-Y trace-header fields `cdp`, `cdpx`, and `cdpy` provide?
cards.back: `cdp` = the CMP/CDP number (trace identity along the line); `cdpx`/`cdpy` = the trace's plan coordinates (X, Y) that place it in the Engineering Frame.

cards.front: In the seismic horizons GeoJSON, what does the `twt_s` property carry and what does converting it require?
cards.back: `twt_s` is the **two-way time per LineString vertex** — the vertical position in *time* (the depth proxy). Converting it to depth requires a velocity model. The LineString itself is the horizon's plan-view trace (X, Y per CMP).

cards.front: After ingestion, what primitive does a reflection SEG-Y become?
cards.back: A `PropertyModel` with **`support = "section"`** — a 2-D vertical *curtain* along the shot line (leading axis = TWT/depth, second = along-line distance). With inline/crossline geometry it becomes a 3-D velocity `volume`. Sibling horizons become `Feature`s of type `horizon`.

cards.front: Define seismic refraction, first break, and head wave.
cards.back: **Refraction** uses **first breaks** — the earliest energy at each geophone. Some energy travels down to a fast layer, runs *along* its top (the **head wave**), and resurfaces. Timing first arrival vs offset recovers shallow velocity structure.

cards.front: On a refraction traveltime-vs-offset curve, what arrives first at short vs long offset, and what does each segment's slope mean?
cards.back: At short offset the **direct wave** arrives first; past a crossover distance the faster **head wave** overtakes it. Each segment's slope is $1/V$ (slowness), so increasing apparent velocity reveals a faster-with-depth layered earth.

cards.front: State the eikonal equation and what it governs.
cards.back: $|\nabla T| = \dfrac{1}{V_p}$. It governs how the first-arrival traveltime field $T(\mathbf{x})$ propagates through a velocity model: the travel-time gradient everywhere equals the local slowness. Solved by fast-marching (conceptually like Dijkstra on a velocity grid).

cards.front: What tool solves the eikonal equation in the T1 refraction forward, and what makes its picks physically sensible?
cards.back: **pykonal** solves the eikonal through the truth $V_p$ model along a refraction spread, giving first-breaks that are monotone in offset with the direct/head-wave crossover appearing naturally. Output: SEG-Y (one Ricker-pulse trace per geophone) + a CSV of picks.

cards.front: Define microseismic monitoring and the magnitude range involved.
cards.back: Recording the *natural* seismic energy — tiny earthquakes (typically magnitude $<0$ to ~2, too small to feel) that rock emits when it fractures or slips. Locating those 'pops' in space and time maps a growing fracture network.

cards.front: Why is microseismic the flagship dataset for EGS (the three-ingredient rule)?
cards.back: Geothermal needs **heat + fluid + permeability** in the same place. Permeability is the hardest to image; MT and boreholes hint at heat/fluid. In EGS you inject high-pressure fluid to crack hot rock, and each crack pops as a micro-earthquake — so microseismic is the closest thing to **watching permeability being created live**.

cards.front: State the Gutenberg–Richter law and define its terms.
cards.back: $\log_{10} N = a - b\,M$, where $N$ = number of events with magnitude $\ge M$, $a$ = overall productivity, and $b$ = the **b-value** (slope, typically $\approx 1$, meaning each unit drop in $M$ gives ~10× more events).

cards.front: What does the b-value indicate, and what is the completeness magnitude $M_c$?
cards.back: A **larger $b$** means relatively more small events (often gentle, fluid-driven cracking); a **smaller $b$** can flag larger, more worrying events. **$M_c$** is the completeness magnitude — a detection floor below which the network misses events.

cards.front: How does the synthetic generator draw magnitudes and locate microseismic events?
cards.back: Magnitudes are drawn from Gutenberg–Richter by inverse-CDF: $M = M_c - \log_{10}(U)/b$ for uniform $U$. Each event is located with an **error that grows with depth** (deeper = farther from the surface array = fuzzier), carried forward as honest uncertainty.

cards.front: What is QuakeML, and why does the forward zero out its lat/lon?
cards.back: **QuakeML** is the standard XML schema for earthquake catalogs (events, origins, magnitudes). Its lat/lon are geographic, but the project's events live in a local **Engineering Frame** — so lat/lon are zeroed and the real Engineering $(x,y)$ is stored in a sibling CSV that the adapter joins by `id`.

cards.front: How is time represented in the QuakeML catalog, and why?
cards.back: As an **explicit ISO-8601 UTC timestamp** (e.g. `2026-01-01T03:00:00.000000Z`). The data model fixes time as a leading `t` axis with real UTC epochs, not project-relative offsets.

cards.front: After ingestion, what primitive does a microseismic QuakeML+CSV become?
cards.back: A single `Feature` of `feature_type = "pointCloud"`: a GeoJSON `MultiPoint` of $[x,y,z]$ whose `props` carry parallel **time** (ISO-8601 UTC) and **magnitude** arrays, with `dims: [x, y, z, t, mag]` — the mandated **4-D point cloud**. If the CSV is missing it falls back to QuakeML depth alone and warns (never fabricating coordinates).

cards.front: What problem does the synthetic data generator solve that real field data can't?
cards.back: With real data you can never compute the error of a fusion/inversion result because the true earth is unknown — nobody has dug up a cubic kilometre of crust to check. The generator builds a fake planet whose every property is known exactly, so recovery error becomes a simple subtraction (truth minus result).

cards.front: In programmer terms, what is the synthetic data generator?
cards.back: A property-based test harness for geophysics: you write a declarative spec (input), the system computes a known-correct ground truth, runs many lossy encoders (survey forward models) over it, and later asserts the decoders (inversion/fusion) reconstruct the original within tolerance. The ground truth is the test oracle.

cards.front: Why are geophysical inverse problems described as 'non-unique'?
cards.back: Many different earths produce nearly the same surface measurements — analogous to how many different source files compress to nearly the same lossy JPEG. So a surface measurement alone can't uniquely identify the rock underground, creating a credibility problem for any inferred result.

cards.front: What is an inverse problem in geophysics?
cards.back: You measure effects at the surface and try to infer the cause (the rock) underground. It's the reverse of forward modelling, and it is non-unique — multiple subsurface configurations can explain the same surface data.

cards.front: What three roles does the synthetic data generator play at once?
cards.back: (1) Day-one data — every viewer pixel and fusion result before real surveys exist; (2) End-to-end exercise of ingestion — emits the same native formats real instruments do, going through the normal adapters; (3) Scoring oracle — true property volumes are saved alongside measured files so results can be scored.

cards.front: Does ingestion ever see the synthetic truth data?
cards.back: No. The generator is just another data source feeding ingestion — not a privileged path. It happens to know the truth, but ingestion only reads the measured/ files; the truth/ volumes are kept aside purely for scoring.

cards.front: What is the single most important design rule of the synthetic data generator?
cards.back: 'One geology → all properties.' Properties are never authored independently; you author one geology and derive every geophysical property (resistivity, density, susceptibility, velocity, etc.) from it via rock physics.

cards.front: Why must all properties be derived from one geology rather than painted separately?
cards.back: Fusion's whole point is that methods agree where they physically should. If you painted resistivity, density, etc. separately, nothing forces the low-resistivity blob to coincide with the low-density blob — fusion would test your painting skill, not the algorithm. Deriving from one source guarantees co-location by construction.

cards.front: In a hot, saline, fractured, clay-altered geothermal zone, what four geophysical signatures appear simultaneously?
cards.back: More conductive / low resistivity (salt water + clay conduct), less dense / low density (porous + fractured), magnetically quiet / low susceptibility (alteration destroys magnetite), and seismically slow / low Vp (cracks + fluid slow sound).

cards.front: What are the two intermediate fields L and S the generator builds before properties?
cards.back: Lithology L(x,y,z) — an integer rock-type label per voxel (a categorical enum: alluvium, volcanics, etc.). State S(x,y,z) — a continuous per-voxel struct of conditions (temperature, porosity, water saturation, salinity, alteration fraction, fracture density) that modulate rock physics within a lithology.

cards.front: What kind of geological event changes L versus S?
cards.back: A fault that juxtaposes two rock units changes L (the categorical map). A hydrothermal upflow that heats, alters, and fractures whatever rock it passes through changes S (the continuous condition). The geothermal target is primarily a state (S) perturbation.

cards.front: What is the truth grid resolution, and why is it finer than any survey?
cards.back: Typically ~50 m laterally and 20 m vertically — finer than any survey can resolve. The truth is authored at full sharpness so each forward model can then degrade it to its own physical resolution, ensuring no survey ever sees more detail than physics allows.

cards.front: What is the deterministic compilation order from spec to properties?
cards.back: surface DEM → stack layers → cut faults/insert intrusions → L(x,y,z); geotherm → blend anomaly + clay-cap perturbations → S(x,y,z); then rock physics maps (L,S) → {ρ, χ, resistivity, η, Vp, Vs, T, φ}. Everything is reproducible from (spec, seed).

cards.front: What guarantees byte-identical synthetic output across runs?
cards.back: Reproducibility from (spec, seed): the same spec and seed give byte-identical output. Seeded correlated random fields add texture, and numpy.random.SeedSequence spawns one independent sub-stream per method so methods are both reproducible and independent.

cards.front: List the authored truth properties with their symbols and units.
cards.back: Density ρ (kg/m³), magnetic susceptibility χ (SI, dimensionless), resistivity/conductivity ρr/σ (Ω·m / S·m⁻¹), chargeability η (mV/V), P/S-wave velocity Vp,Vs (m/s), temperature T (°C), porosity φ (fraction). Resistivity, chargeability, and Vs are derived, never authored directly.

cards.front: Which property is 'the target' of geothermal exploration, and what drives it?
cards.back: Temperature T (°C). It is a state-field property, driven by the background geotherm plus the hydrothermal anomaly. It's consumed by heat-flow and well-temperature methods.

cards.front: What is Archie's law (modified with a clay term) for resistivity?
cards.back: 1/ρr = (φ^m · Sw^n)/(a·ρw) + clayCond(alterationFrac, T). The first term is the Archie pore-fluid conduction path; the second is a parallel surface-conduction path through clay. It links bulk resistivity to porosity and salty-water content.

cards.front: In Archie's law, what are m, n, and a, and their typical values?
cards.back: m is the cementation exponent (~2), n the saturation exponent (~2), and a the tortuosity constant (~1) — empirical Archie parameters. φ is porosity, Sw water saturation, ρw pore-water resistivity (drops sharply with salinity and temperature).

cards.front: Why does the geothermal clay cap form such a strong, shallow conductor?
cards.back: Clay surfaces conduct electricity independently of the pore fluid, adding a parallel conduction path (the clay term in modified Archie's law). Combined with hot + saline + porous, this makes the clay cap a strong, diagnostic shallow conductor — the electromagnetic signature of a geothermal system.

cards.front: What is the porosity-mixing formula for density?
cards.back: ρ = (1−φ)·ρ_grain(unit) + φ·ρ_fluid — solid grains of the rock unit diluted by light pore fluid. Higher porosity lowers density; intrusions are dense, alluvium is light.

cards.front: Why does a hydrothermal upflow read as a magnetic LOW, not a high?
cards.back: χ = χ_base(unit)·(1 − alterationFrac). Hydrothermal alteration chemically destroys magnetite, so the upflow's susceptibility drops toward zero. Magnetics therefore sees a hole, not heat — fusion must infer the heat from the joint pattern of methods.

cards.front: What is the seismic velocity softening formula, and why does Vp/Vs flag fluid?
cards.back: Vp = Vp,base·(1 − k_φ·φ − k_fr·fractureDensity): porosity and fractures soften velocity. Saturation raises Vp but barely moves Vs, so the ratio Vp/Vs flags the presence of fluid.

cards.front: What are the three universal degradations applied in every forward model?
cards.back: (1) Acquisition geometry — survey layout limiting where you sample (sparse/irregular sampling); (2) Resolution/DOI — method-specific physical blur, a depth-worsening low-pass filter; (3) Noise — additive/multiplicative method-appropriate noise from a seeded RNG. Each forward model is a lossy encoder.

cards.front: What is depth of investigation (DOI)?
cards.back: The depth below which a method effectively can't see — its signal has decayed into the noise; the geophysical analogue of a sensor's range. E.g. ERT's DOI ≈ 0.15–0.2 × array length, while MT (long-period natural signals) reaches kilometres deep but only smoothly.

cards.front: Why do the MT and ERT methods correctly disagree at depth?
cards.back: The shallow clay cap is sharply imaged by ERT but only smoothly by MT; the deep reservoir conductor lies below ERT's DOI (invisible to it) and is MT's domain. Their disagreement at depth is physically correct and becomes a genuine fusion test case.

cards.front: Why is seismic nearly blind to the temperature field?
cards.back: Seismic reflectivity comes from impedance contrasts (layer contacts, faults), so it images the faulted geometry/structure but not fluid or heat directly. The fluid cue must come from Vp/Vs via logs or refraction.

cards.front: What are the two fidelity tiers, and what distinguishes them?
cards.back: T0 'plausible' — degrade-the-truth: apply resolution kernel + DOI mask + noise and project to geometry (plus analytic potential-field forwards); ms–s to run. T1 'rigorous' — a real PDE forward solver (harmonica, SimPEG/PyGIMLi, empymod, devito/pykonal); seconds–minutes. Both implement the same ForwardModel interface, so upgrading is a drop-in swap.

cards.front: Why build T0 (plausible) for every method first, then promote T1 selectively?
cards.back: T0-everything-first proves the full vertical slice end-to-end (synth → ingest → store → view → fuse) before any expensive solver exists. T1 is then promoted where realism most gates fusion — MT, gravity (deep, smooth, non-unique hard cases) and seismic (sharp structure). All currently-shipped forwards are T0.

cards.front: What is a 'scenario' in the synthetic data generator?
cards.back: A named, self-contained synthetic survey play: a declarative SceneSpec (the earth) plus an Acquisition (what gets collected). The two are decoupled so the same earth can be surveyed densely or sparsely. Examples: unit-cube-v1 (CI smoke test) and great-basin-v1 (flagship).

cards.front: What is the great-basin-v1 flagship scenario?
cards.back: A Basin & Range / Great Basin extensional hydrothermal play (canonical Western-US setting, aligned with Nevada/Milford-FORGE): alluvium-filled valley over volcanics/carbonate over granite basement, a 60°-dip range-front normal fault (~700 m throw) that is both master structure and fluid conduit, and a fault-controlled hydrothermal upflow (~220 °C).

cards.front: In great-basin-v1, how is the geothermal target represented in the spec?
cards.back: As a state (S) perturbation — a 'hydrothermal-plume' anomaly controlled by the range-front conduit fault: tempPeak 220 °C, alterationFrac 0.6, porosityBoost 0.04, salinityTDS 8000, fractureDensity 0.5, plus a shallow clay-cap conductor. It is hot + altered + porous + saline + fractured.

cards.front: What background geotherm does great-basin-v1 use, and why is it high?
cards.back: Surface temp 15 °C with a 45 °C/km gradient — Basin & Range runs hot due to high heat flow and extensional tectonics. This sets the background T in the state field S before the anomaly is blended in.

cards.front: What is the textbook joint geophysical signature great-basin-v1 produces?
cards.back: An MT/EM conductor + a magnetic low (alteration destroys magnetite) + the gravity expression of the basin/fault + seismic-imaged structure + a hot well. Each method earns its keep and the combination creates genuine non-uniqueness challenges for fusion.

cards.front: What is the critical structural rule of the output scenario folder?
cards.back: measured/ is the ONLY thing ingestion reads; truth/ is never ingested — it would be cheating, since truth is the answer key. measured/ holds native-format files (SEG-Y, EDI, LAS, GeoTIFF, QuakeML…); truth/ holds property/lithology/state zarr volumes and true features used only for validation.

cards.front: What does the truth/ folder contain, and in what coordinate system?
cards.back: properties.zarr (ρ, χ, resistivity, η, Vp, Vs, T, φ on the truth grid in Engineering coords), lithology.zarr and state.zarr (the L and S fields), and features.geojson (true faults/horizons/anomaly solids). A manifest.json records seed, versions, checksums, and per-file provenance.

cards.front: Why are measured/ files written in real instrument native formats (SEG-Y, EDI, LAS…)?
cards.back: So they are byte-compatible with what the ingestion adapters parse from real instruments — there's no back-door into the data store. If a format round-trips through synthgen and ingestion, the ingestion contract is proven end-to-end.

cards.front: Why is no number in this geophysics platform a true 'measurement' of the rock?
cards.back: Every value (resistivity from inversion, temperature from rock physics, favorability score) is an *inference* from indirect surface observations, not a direct reading. They are estimates of something you cannot see, and must carry error bars.

cards.front: What is the single most important engineering principle of the uncertainty system?
cards.back: Never let the type system or the UI imply more certainty than the data supports. A value without its uncertainty is a lie of omission, and the platform refuses to ship one.
